[[RunningHead]]

Courtship Letters of Freud and Martha Bernays

Sigmund Freud, Martha Bernays: Die Brautbriefe, Band 2. Unser “Roman in Fortsetzunge” [The Letters during Their Engagement, Vol. 2. Our “Novel in Installments”]. Edited by Gerhard Fichtner, Ilse Grubrich-Simitis, Albrecht Hirschmüller, and Wolfgang Kloft. Frankfurt a.M.: S. Fischer Verlag, 2013, 613 pp., ﻿€48.00.

Rita Teusch

Rita Teusch

129 Mt. Auburn Street
Cambridge, MA 02138
E-mail: ritateusch@verizon.net

Rita Teusch   Training and Supervising Analyst, Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; Instructor in Psychiatry (Psychology), Cambridge Health Alliance, Harvard Medical School.

In 2011 the first volume of the unedited courtship letters between Sigmund Freud and his fiancée Martha Bernays was published in Germany. With a German title translating as Be Mine as I Imagine You, it contains 230 letters written by the couple during the first year of their engagement. Volume 1 was reviewed by Christian Maetzener in JAPA 60/5 (2012) and by Riccardo Steiner (2013), whose broad review for IJP includes a wealth of historical and contextual information pertinent to understanding the complex emotional and sociocultural situation of the couple.

The second volume of the Freud-Bernays correspondence was published last year. Its title, Unser “Roman in Fortsetzungen,” in English Our “Novel in Installments,” is a phrase used humorously by Martha in describing her eager anticipation of Freud's letters. Volume 2, which covers the first six months of the second year of the engagement (July through December 1883), includes 277 letters between Freud and his fiancée.

Three additional volumes of the correspondence are to be published in the next few years, each title a quote from the letters reflecting a predominant psychological theme in the period covered: Volume 3: Waiting Quietly and with Surrender, Waiting Struggling and with Exasperation. Volume 4: Traces of Our Complicated Existence. Volume 5: Being with You as You Are.

As Steiner (2013) notes, Freud and Bernays lived in a small community of Jewish immigrants in Vienna.

Journal Watch

T. Insel, B. Cuthbert, M. Garvey, R. Heinssen, D.S. Pine, K. Quinn, C. Sanislow, & P. Wang (2010). Research domain criteria (RDoC): Toward a new classification framework for research on mental disorders.American Journal of Psychiatry167:748-751.

Tiziano Colibazzi

Tiziano Colibazzi

Tiziano Colibazzi  

Debate over the merits and demerits of DSM-5 has been the talk of the town since release of the manual in May of last year. Despite all the anticipation, no Copernican revolution has occurred. DSM still follows the medical model of disease, defining nosological categories on the basis of clusters of symptoms. These categories are thought to represent disorders with a specific longitudinal course, prognosis, and (to a degree) response to treatment. Many of us, however, may be less familiar with an effort, equally important, that is slowly but steadily informing the way research in neuroscience and psychiatry is being conceptualized and carried out.

In 2010 NIMH released a blueprint, titled Research Domain Criteria (RDoC), intended to serve as a framework for neuroscience research. RDoC takes an approach different from that of DSM. It assumes (1) that mental disorders can be explained in terms of brain circuits and (2) that abnormalities of these circuits are identifiable and will lead to the discovery of “biosignatures”, ultimately to be integrated with clinical data. This effort should lead to a classification system based, as is generally the norm in medicine generally, on etiology and/or pathophysiology. Unlike DSM, RDoC is not yet a diagnostic system; rather, it is an attempt to create one.

In a major change, RDoC shifts the pivot of research away from symptoms and to the level of brain circuitry in an effort to identify circuit-based functional constructs. These functional constructs are grouped under broader domains of brain function.

But what exactly are these constructs and domains? Let's take the example of cognition. Cognition constitutes a domain, like other brain functions identified by RDoC (e.g., negative affect, social processes positive affect, and regulatory systems). The domain labeled “cognition” subsumes a variety of constructs that can be the object of investigation: attention, long-term memory, cognitive control, working memory, executive function, and perception.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Continuity and Change Redux

Bonnie Litowitz

Bonnie Litowitz

Bonnie Litowitz  

It is an honor to have been chosen as the next Editor of JAPA. I am mindful of the five distinguished editors who have preceded me and have worked closely with two of them: Arnold Richards and Steven Levy. I have learned much from them about journal publishing but, more important, I have absorbed the high standards of scholarship and openness to innovation that they have set for JAPA.

These are challenging times in which to produce a high-quality scholarly journal in a field such as psychoanalysis, where no one theoretical perspective dominates among diverse approaches and where each has its own dedicated journal. Although some have decried the loss of a single theoretical perspective as the Balkanization of psychoanalysis (Stepansky 2009), I consider this diversity a positive source of energy for the growth of our field. In such a fertile landscape, I envision JAPA's unique position as the agora—the marketplace—where the best of different perspectives can meet and engage in civil discourse for the advancement of our understanding of the human mind, its relation to other minds, and its historical and cultural context.

In my introduction to JAPA's 50th Anniversary issue (Litowitz 2002) I cited the challenge of “continuity and change,” comparing our field to an organism that must adapt to current conditions or die out. Selecting a female psychoanalyst with an academic, nonmedical background as the next JAPA Editor is evidence of the changes already happening in our profession, as well as an acknowledgment of the need for our discipline to compete in the marketplace of ideas among both clinical and academic scholars.

Psychoanalysis is a unique clinical method of inquiry into the human condition. A major goal for JAPA will be to seek ways to share the significant findings from our unique database with scholars whose findings rely on other methodologies. Only through an open exchange of ideas can we establish consilience across disciplines or identify and eliminate obstacles that impede our working together productively.

[[RunningHead]]

Poster Summaries: Poster Summaries from the 2014 Poster Session of the American Psychoanalytic Association

Impact of Object Relations and Impulsivity on Persistent Suicidal Behavior

Katie Lewis , Kevin Meehan , Jane Tillman , Nicole Cain , Philip Wong , A. Clemence and Jennifer Stevens

Jennifer Stevens

Albany Medical College
Department of Psychiatry
South Clinical Campus, MC 164
25 Hackett Boulevard
Albany, NY 12208
E-mail: katiecooperlewis@gmail.com

Jennifer Stevens  

Suicide has been identified consistently over the past decade as one of the leading causes of death in the United States. Between the years 2009-2011, suicide was estimated to be one of the top five leading causes of death for people between the ages of five and forty-four (Murphy, Xu, and Kochanek 2012).

485

Clinical populations are at an especially elevated risk for suicide (Ortigo, Westen, and Bradley 2009), and those with a history of suicide attempts are more likely to make an attempt than those with no suicidal history (Joiner et al. 2005).

Several studies have identified impulsivity as an important risk factor for suicide (e.g., Nock and Kessler 2006; Witte et al. 2008), although the exact role of impulsivity in the development of and capacity for suicidal behavior remains unclear. Hakansson et al. (2011) found that subjects who made multiple suicide attempts throughout their lifetime reported higher impulsivity-related traits, such as difficulty controlling anger and an inability to maintain concentration. However, Nakagawa et al. (2009) found that the level of planning for a recent suicide attempt was unrelated to trait levels of impulsivity—that is, individuals who attempted suicide with little forethought had similar levels of impulsivity compared to those who spent a significant amount of time planning their attempt.

Research has also shown that poor quality of object relations, defined as an internal understanding of the world and others that is poorly differentiated, illogical, or polarized in affective tone, is associated with psychiatric disturbance in several domains, including suicidal behavior (Twomey, Kaslow, and Croft 2000). Kaslow et al. (1998), for example, found that subjects with a recent suicide attempt demonstrated lower complexity and more malevolent affective quality of internal representations in comparison to subjects with no history of suicidal behavior, as well as a lower emotional investment in interpersonal relationships.

The capacity to manage impulsive urges may be related to underlying quality of object representations, in that both are associated with emotional regulation capacities. Several studies have shown that a specific type of impulsivity, in which impulsive behavior arises only in the context of overwhelming negative affect (“negative urgency”), is related to suicidal behavior (Bornavalova et al. 2011; Klonsky and May 2010). These findings support other research demonstrating the importance of loss of self-regulatory functions (indicated by loss of impulse control) in “hot,” emotionally-activating situations (Cain et al. 2013; Meehan et al. 2013; Mischel and Ayduk 2002) and confirm that such a response pattern can facilitate engagement in suicidal behavior.

Journal Watch

J.P. Barber, M.S. Barrett, R. Gallop, R.A. Rynn, & K. Rickels (2012). Short-term dynamic psychotherapy versus pharmacotherapy for major depressive disorder: A randomized, placebo-controlled trial.Journal of Clinical Psychiatry73:66-73.

Sargam Jain

Sargam Jain

Sargam Jain  

Melancholia has always been on the minds of philosophers and healers. Hippocrates, Sufi mystics, Romantic poets, Buddhists, and Ayurvedic practitioners have described it as an inexplicable despondency, attributed variously to a loss of religious faith, overinvestment in materialism, or the dysregulation of internal somatic processes. In a way, the history of melancholy is the history of humankind's struggle to describe and treat suffering. This tradition has not ended with the modern-day equivalent of melancholy, major depressive disorder.

Despite the diversity of explanations, melancholia was always understood as a complex process through which the psychological experience of sadness was materialized. Similarly, major depressive disorder is understood as a psychobiological disorder that includes a sad or depressed mood with somatic disturbances of appetite, energy, and sleep. However, we continue to struggle to understand why people become depressed, in the hope we might better heal them. Complicating matters is the fact that how one's suffering is expressed varies significantly across race, culture, and class.

Expressions of depression are overdetermined by the personal meanings individuals attach to their symptoms. Does the personal context of depressive symptoms confound today's standardized treatments for depression? And the questions of evolutionary psychiatry also cloud the picture: Is depression an appropriate response to external conditions that provoke psychic pain? Does this imply the need for social treatments as opposed to individualized ones?

The article under review speaks to some of these ancient questions. The study is unique in that it is the first attempt to compare the efficacy of short-term dynamic therapy (specifically, supportive-expressive treatment) to contemporary antidepressant therapy, as well as to a placebo control for the treatment of depression. Also notable is that the study population was recruited from a poor urban population with a large minority component (52%)

[[RunningHead]]

Introduction to Translations

Bonnie Litowitz

Bonnie Litowitz

Bonnie Litowitz  

We are pleased to inaugurate a new feature for JAPA Online readers worldwide. In collaboration with colleagues in Seoul, Korea, and Wuhan, China, we will be presenting translations in Korean and Mandarin of articles that have been published in JAPA. Papers will be selected and translated into Korean by Sunghae Min and selected and translated into Mandarin by Ping Yu and colleagues. (Links to the original articles will appear for each paper.)

We are grateful to our colleagues in Seoul and Wuhan for their commitment to disseminating articles by American psychoanalytic writers and, in this way, to encouraging dialogue across cultures on psychoanalytic theory and practice.

K. Bertsch, M. Gamer, B. Schmidt, I. Schmidinger, S. Walther, T. Kastel, C. Buchel, G. Domes, & S. Herpertz (2013). Oxytocin and reduction of social threat hypersensitivity in women with borderline personality disorder.American Journal of Psychiatry170:1169-1177.

Sargam Jain

Sargam Jain

Sargam Jain  

710

Object relations theory accounts for the tendency of people with borderline personality pathology to engage in projection and projective identification—the intolerable within is denied and attributed to the other. However, the question of how brain produces mind remains a mystery. What is happening on a neurological level when the borderline person experiences negative affects? Where does projection begin, biologically speaking?

The authors of this paper postulate that it might begin with the initial eye movements one makes when assessing facial expression. Various studies have shown that we pay attention to the eyes in particular when looking for social cues. In addition, the eyes are thought to be the most emotionally salient part of a threatening expression. It is possible, then, that a component of the borderline's bias toward negative social information, angry reactivity, and attribution of threat to others is due to a tendency to excessively focus on the other's eyes. In essence, the borderline person is “looking for” aggression that isn't there. This excessive focus could lead to hyperactivity in areas of the brain responsible for assigning meaning to emotional stimuli—in this case, negative meanings. If this focus could be modulated, the authors believe it could in turn modulate the reactive, angry responses borderline patients tend to have toward perceived threats.

Oxytocin, a neuropeptide, is known to modulate social behavior and functions in “coding” the salience of social information, attaching affect to phenomena within the amygdala. Individual and situational differences impact the effect of oxytocin administration in individuals, though overall it seems to optimize the processing of social cues. For this reason, the authors chose to study the effects of intranasal oxytocin administration on facial threat processing in borderline individuals. Groups of healthy female control subjects and borderline women were divided into groups receiving placebo or 26 IU of nasally administered oxytocin. Forty minutes after receiving either placebo drug or oxytocin, subjects were asked to classify 72 briefly presented faces (both male and female) that unambiguously depicted angry, happy, or fearful expressions. These faces were presented at varying fixation points—the subjects saw either the eyes or the mouth of the image first. During this task, functional MRI (fMRI) scanning was done, detecting changes in blood oxygen levels within the amygdala. Whole-brain fMRI imaging was not performed. Gaze behavior was also tracked, using a camera mounted under the subject's right eye.

[[RunningHead]]

Quartiers Aux Rues Sans Nom (Neighborhoods of Nameless Streets). By Dominique Scarfone. Paris: Éditions de l'Olivier, 2012, 114 pp., €12.00.

Marie-Ange Pongis-Khandjian

Marie-Ange Pongis-Khandjian

1180 Avenue Murray
Quebec, QC G1S 386
CANADA
E-mail: m-a.pongis-khandjian@crsfa.ulaval.ca

Marie-Ange Pongis-Khandjian   Psychologist and psychoanalyst, Quebec Psychoanalytic Society; Secretary, Canadian Psychoanalytic Society.

Browsing through the six short but dense essays that make up Dominique Scarfone's Quartiers aux rues sans nom, the reader drifts between psychoanalytic and poetic reverie. While strolling through transitional areas, in between the external and internal worlds, through the cracks of rigorous scientific theory and unconscious intuitive practice, the reader is invited to think in a manner that puts his thought to work far away from traditionalism, dogma, or bureaucratic routine.

The title essay, “Neighborhoods with Nameless Streets,” is about the psychoanalytic method. Here Scarfone describes some unconscious psychic lands as gray areas, badly mapped, where representations are transformed by secondary repression or erased, much as the citizens of Venice and Prague once erased street names to fool invaders. Nonetheless, streets still existed and one could find one's way. However, the psychoanalytic approach eventually leads us to other kinds of regions. Something is “presented” that has no link with psychic representations. Streets here were never named; in fact there are hardly any streets, just some uncertain traces on footpaths. In the absence of words, both analyst and patient are confronted with their infantile aphasia. Transference emerges where words are missing: close to an action, it has to be embodied to become a living understanding of a state and an experience, rather than of an intellectual comprehension.

“Lunar Suite,” the longest and most personal text in the book, is paradoxically based on the threadbare question “Is psychoanalysis an art or a science?” Using a clinical vignette, Scarfone illustrates the process of creation, rather than construction, when confronted with the unforeseen. Allowing us to enter his intimate experience, he reveals childhood memories that echo with his patients, their infantile experiences, and his blind spots. We see him at work concerned about an intense transference that leads to an impasse and finally an enactment. He illustrates this unexpected irruption into the transferential storm, setting the analytic scene truly ablaze. The analyst is confronted with the paradox of having to respond without responding.

Il Bambino e le Sue Relazioni: Attaccamento e Individualitá Tra Teoria e Osservazione (The Child and His Relations: Attachment and Individuality between Theory and Observation). By Cristina Riva Crugnola. Milan: Routledge, 2007, 286 pp., $19.55 paperback.

Elisabetta Superchi

Elisabetta Superchi

11910 Malvern Road
Shaker Heights, OH 44122
E-mail: e.superchi@gmail.com

Elisabetta Superchi   Child analyst and Associate, Hannah Perkins Center for Child Development.

In The Child and His Relations Cristina Riva Crugnola examines the early stages of relational development. The book, written in a densely informative style, offers a comprehensive view of theoretical and observational work in the field of infant research. The author's integration of viewpoints usually regarded as divergent, as well as of experimental and clinical research, is the book's greatest contribution.

In the book's first part Crugnola establishes the background for what will follow, unifying decades of studies in attachment theory, intersubjectivity, infant research, self-regulation, neurobiology, and object relations theory. In the section's last chapter she also integrates aspects of research in the field of ego psychology.

In the second part Crugnola presents a longitudinal study (carried out with A. Albizzati, C. Caprin, S. Gazzotti, S. Sagliaschi, and M. Walder) conducted with thirty-four mother-infant pairs from birth to three years. The study provides the basis for her interventions in the cases described in the book's final chapter.

In examining the theoretical background, Crugnola provides the reader a chronologically organized review of the concepts and theoretical models uniquely integrated in her work. In doing so she presents a unified framework of child development that accords with the clinical experience of any psychotherapist. She begins by bringing together the infant research of recent decades, with an emphasis on emotional aspects of the child's socio-interactive skills.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Introduction: The Theorist and the Theory

Bonnie Litowitz

Bonnie Litowitz

Bonnie Litowitz  

To see in the day or in the year a symbol

Of the days of man and of his years,

To transmute the outrage of the years

Into a music, a murmur of voices and a symbol …

—Jorge Luis Borges, “Ars Poetica”

We all agree that as psychoanalysts we are working in a time of theoretical pluralism. We strive to understand whether we are awaiting the arrival of a new paradigm that will incorporate the best of current theoretical perspectives into a grand, inclusive theory (Kuhn 1962), or if a multiplicity of perspectives is itself the new paradigm, comparable, for example, to complementarity in quantum physics?1 This would seem, therefore, an appropriate time to consider whence theories have arisen in the past.

We are accustomed to viewing changes in psychoanalytic theory as arising from our work with patients. In Kuhnian fashion, we change or extend our theoretical perspectives when we find that existing theory cannot fit all the data we encounter in our work. Freud himself set the example for this approach as he changed his theory throughout his life, adjusting and reformulating to explain expanding clinical evidence.2

An alternative perspective on how theoretical change arises is suggested by Robert Wallerstein (2014) in his recent essay on Erik Erikson. Wallerstein's thesis is that, along with such objective data, we should also take into consideration the personal struggles of major theorists themselves as they work in and react to a particular sociocultural context: “great issues of a particular time and place, as experienced by sensitive

1“Complementarity can be formulated without explicit reference to physics, to wit, as two aspects of a description that are mutually exclusive yet both necessary for a full understanding of what is to be described. Bohr attempted to apply complementarity in this broad sense to other fields, such as psychology, biology, and anthropology” (Pais 1991, p. 24).2Freud did not coordinate changes in technique with changes in theory. In consequence, metapsychology tended to float free from technique. By contrast, a common practice in theory construction after Freud is for changes in theory and technique to go hand in hand.

JAPA Prize Announcment

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association

JAPA Prize

The $1000 JAPA Prize is awarded to the author of the best paper published in the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association during the previous year. All publications, including review articles, research articles, essays and book essays, may be considered for the award. Candidates for the award are nominated by the members of the JAPA Editorial Board, with the final decision being made by the Editor-in-Chief.

JAPA New Author Prize

JAPA's $500 New Author Prize recognizes significant contributions to the journal by new authors. It is awarded for the best published manuscript by a new author, and is presented at the Winter Meetings of the American Psychoanalytic Association.

A new author is considered to be someone who has not previously published in JAPA or any other major psychoanalytic journal and whose psychoanalytic career is in its early stages. A mentoring/editorial group has been established to review submissions by new authors and assist in developing manuscripts for publication in JAPA.

Manuscripts must be submitted via the JAPA submission process, using the online system at http://mc.manuscriptcentral.com/japa. At the time of submission, authors should indicate in the cover letter that the paper is to be considered for the New Author Prize, and may ask that it be directed to the mentoring/editorial group for assistance in manuscript development. Final decision about publication remains with the Editorial Board and the Editor-in-Chief, as does selection of the annual prize-winning paper.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Paolo Migone's “What Does ‘Brief’ Mean?”

Marcia Cavell

Marcia Cavell

E-mail: mcavell@earthlink.net

Marcia Cavell  

I write regarding “The Politics of Psychoanalytic Lexicography,” by Elizabeth Auchincloss and Eslee Samberg (JAPA 623). The authors write that “‘Drive,’ also sometimes referred to as ‘instinct’ … is the mental representation of an endogenous motivational force … that stimulates mental activity …” (p. 527). It should be noted that “drive” is one of the several concepts that belong in the conceptual net Freud called “psychic energy,” which he derived from the constancy principle in physics. Freud postulated that there is psychic energy, on a par with gravitational energy, kinetic energy, electrical energy, etc., meaning that psychological activities like willing, deciding, meaning, feeling, etc. can be quantified, just like physical energies. No such energy has ever been defined, and there are very strong conceptual reasons why it can never be, reasons having to do with the irreducibility of the mental to the physical. It is an important point because this irreducibility means that psychoanalysis will never be a science on the order of physics, as Freud had hoped.

Marcia Cavell

E-mail: mcavell@earthlink.net

NP18

Language in the Analytic Hour: Clinical Discussion of Harris, Kirshner, and Spivak

Ilene Dyller

Ilene Dyller

1315 Walnut Street, Suite 801
Philadelphia, PA 19107
E-mail: idyller@aol.com

Ilene Dyller  

The papers in this section address the question “How does talking cure?” Each paper, on its own, presents a complex, nuanced exploration, and it would be impossible in a brief discussion to do justice both to the intricacy and sophistication of the material and to its applicability to clinical practice. I will limit myself, therefore, to a simpler task: applying a central idea from each author to a clinical vignette of my own, with the aim of highlighting the clinical relevance and utility of the idea. I will also point out an area of theoretical disagreement between two of the authors and briefly discuss the clinical implications of this disagreement.

Clinical Vignette

Early in her analysis, Jennifer, a woman in her late twenties, related the following incident. As an anxious young child, Jennifer often turned to her mother, with whom she had a loving relationship, for reassurance to allay a wide range of anxieties. Although her mother was usually able to comfort her, at times she became overwhelmed by her daughter's fears and demands. At the age of six Jennifer needed to have her tonsils removed. Terrified at the prospect, she repeatedly begged not to be subjected to the procedure. Her mother initially tried to reassure her that she would be fine but eventually lost patience and blurted out, “Oh, for heaven's sake, it's just a tonsillectomy!” Jennifer's fear of the procedure was compounded by her recognition that her mother, in this instance, could not handle her daughter's anxiety and demands for reassurance.

Private practice, Philadelphia; candidate, Psychoanalytic Center of Philadelphia.

Submitted for publication July 17, 2014.

[[RunningHead]]

Creativity, Creator

Donald Winnicott Today. Edited by Jan Abram. London: Routledge, 2012, xxx + 478 pp., $165.00 hardcover, $47.95 paperback.

Susan Donner

Susan Donner

21243 Ventura Boulevard, Suite 239
Woodland Hills, CA 91364
E-mail: sldonnermd@gmail.com

Susan Donner   Faculty, New Center for Psychoanalysis, Los Angeles; Assistant Clinical Professor in Psychiatry, UCLA Geffen School of Medicine.

Donald Winnicott Today (2012) is the stimulating product of Jan Abram's mission to demonstrate the importance of D. W. Winnicott's legacy in the history and theory of psychoanalysis. Honorary Archivist of the Winnicott Trust (2002-2011) and author of The Language of Winnicott: A Dictionary of Winnicott's Use of Words (1996), Abram has written several chapters in the book under review, in addition to being editor of the volume. She has carefully selected a handful of Winnicott's talks and notes (including an excerpt of heretofore unpublished material) to introduce chosen secondary works on Winnicott. By using Winnicott's own notes to introduce segments of the book, Abram successfully keeps his voice firmly present throughout. She has republished classic analytic papers by such authors as Thomas Ogden, André Green, and Haydée Faimberg. More recent papers by authors spanning the globe and bridging professional disciplines highlight different aspects of Winnicott and the scope of his influence and inspiration.

Abram proposes a reconsideration of Winnicott's contribution as being revolutionary within the field of psychoanalysis and looks to its reintegration within the historical pantheon of ideas and theories. She places Winnicott in a theoretical lineage other than simply the Independent or Middle British Group and distances him from Melanie Klein and her followers. Citing André Green, she traces his theoretical frame back to Freud's 1895 Project for A Scientific Psychology, though she does not fully explicate the connection in this volume (p. 2). By drawing attention to the matrix between m/other and baby that is co-created, Abram argues, Winnicott has diverged from Freudian instinct theory to emphasize two-person relationships and the environment, and thus lays a foundation for the concept of subjectivity and for later intersubjective theories (p. 2).

The authors in Part 1, Introductory Overviews, delineate Winnicott's distinctive, even revolutionary, contributions.

Psychoanalytic Perspectives

Tormenti di anime: Passioni, sintomi, sogni (Torments of the Soul: Passions, Symptoms, Dreams). By Antonino Ferro. Milan: Raffaello Cortina Editore, 2010, 216 pp., €21.00.

Daria Colombo

Daria Colombo

425 East 79th Street, Suite 1J
New York, NY 10075
E-mail: dcolombo8@gmail.com

Daria Colombo   Faculty, New York Psychoanalytic Institute. Adrienne Harris, Clinical Associate Professor of Psychology, NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis.

In recent years Antonino Ferro's body of work has received increasing recognition throughout the psychoanalytic world. In his latest book, Torments of the Soul: Passions, Symptoms, Dreams,1 he revisits and expands some of his fundamental ideas, making a strong claim for the value of his approach while bringing us ever more vividly into analytic sessions described in a rich and deeply textured narrative. That his text is engaging and frequently absorbing is testimony not only to his skill as a writer, but to his commitment to the importance of narrative theory, which along with semiotics and Bionian ideas, informs his psychoanalytic approach. In this new work, dense with clinical material, psychoanalytic diagrams and schemas, and material drawn from the world of literature, film, and the visual arts (he includes some of Fellini's drawings as well as drawings by patients), Ferro gives us an intimate sense of how he thinks, not only about analysis but about the foundation of human subjectivity and how this translates into clinical work with adults and children.

Ferro has been one of the most creative, prolific, and fecund disciples of Bion, if one can use the word disciple for one so devoted to the destabilizing of orthodoxy and to the privileging of creativity as central, not only to psychoanalysis, but to the human condition generally. Ferro's ideas have become familiar to many of late, and this work serves equally well as either an introduction or an opportunity for deepening one's acquaintance. For Ferro, the analytic session functions as a dream.

1An English translation of this book is scheduled for publication later this year in the New Library of Psychoanalysis: Torments of the Soul: Passions, Symptoms, Dreams (London: Routledge).545

Steven Roose

Steven Roose

Steven Roose  

It is widely acknowledged that whatever misgivings some may have about empirical research, data that support the effectiveness of psychoanalysis can only help our field in this era of evidence-based therapeutics. This is especially so if the study design has the methodological rigor to ensure acceptance of the results beyond the borders of psychoanalysis and if the study compares psychoanalysis to the currently popular briefer-term psychotherapies. However, conducting studies of long-term treatments is a complex venture in myriad ways. Perhaps the biggest challenge is to develop a study design that strikes the right balance between clinical relevance and rigorous methodology. It is not surprising that the number of meaningful outcome studies of psychoanalysis is painfully small, and so when such a study is completed the results should be widely known and discussed among psychoanalysts.

Recently Dorothea Huber and colleagues published the results of a three-year follow-up of the Munich Psychotherapy Study. This study included patients with chronic unipolar depression and randomized them to treatment in one of three conditions: cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT), 40-60 sessions at a frequency of one session a week; psychodynamic therapy (PD), 50-80 sessions also at a frequency of one session a week; or psychoanalysis (PSA), 160-240 sessions at a frequency of two to three sessions a week with the patient lying on the couch (a lesser frequency and shorter duration than is common for psychoanalytic treatments conducted in the United States).

Rebecca Chaplan

Rebecca Chaplan

40 West 77th Street, 1F
New York, NY 10024
E-mail: rc361@columbia.edu

Rebecca Chaplan   Assistant Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Columbia University; Training and Supervising Analyst, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research.

These two extraordinary graphic memoirs, Stitches, by David Small, and Are You My Mother? A Comic Drama, by Alison Bechdel, are about the artist coming of age while trying to understand the forces that shape his or her life and art. In both novels psychoanalysis figures as a lifesaving intervention, portrayed with seriousness rare in popular literature, including love and gratitude to the analysts who figure as characters in the stories. Quite different from each other in style, these are intelligent graphic novels for adults that take psychic development as their main theme. Both are superb examples of the sophisticated narrative possible in this form.

The graphic novel lends itself to representing subjective psychic experience. Like film, it has the advantage of the visual form for depicting fantasy, with the affordances of compositional techniques including framing, sequencing, cutting, and zoom. The ability to convey facial expression and body posture allows for efficient and nuanced communication of affect. In addition, the graphic novel has the benefit of using words in ways that are not possible in film. As in a dream, when there is text in a graphic novel, words are also “things,” and this concreteness opens more possibilities for blending language with the visual image. Freud (1900)

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Speech as the Confluence of Words, Body, and Relationship: Discussion of Harris, Kirshner, and Spivak

Jeanine Vivona

Jeanine Vivona

11 Bala Avenue
Bala Cynwyd, PA 19004
E-mail: jvivona@tcnj.edu

Jeanine Vivona  

I initiated this project with the hope that juxtaposing distinct understandings of the role of speech and words in psychoanalytic treatments would bring to light some of our latent assumptions and assist us in more clearly articulating our beliefs about the therapeutic potentials of language, perhaps even rewarding us with a clearer sense of the nature of language as viewed through the lens of its clinical potential. Those hopes have been realized through the marvelous words of Alan Spivak, Lewis Kirshner, Adrienne Harris, and Ilene Dyller. What immediately strikes me about these papers, so different in content and tone, is the very rich understanding of language at the heart of each. Although the authors contend differently with the heterogeneous nature of speech and language, and with the relationship between language and thought, the unconscious, and the body, none of them dismisses the power of words on the basis of their being abstract symbols. Quite the contrary. Moreover, framing the question of therapeutic action around talking has yielded theorizing that places the relatedness that occurs in speech at the center of this question of therapeutic action, where it belongs. Each author envisions an essential interweaving of language and relationship in speech.

For Spivak, words effect change because they are the medium through which the analyst can do two things simultaneously. First, the analyst uses words to transform the unconscious wishes that fuel the transference into a form that is accessible to the patient's conscious mind; thus, “Psychoanalytic knowing is re-knowing,” or knowing in a new form. Second, speech is a medium through which the analyst relates to the patient in such a way that inspires an experience of being understood and recognized.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Primary/Secondary Process Cognition and Embodied Space

Mike Finn , Morgun Custer , Gyrid Lyon and Michael Nash

Michael Nash

311 Austin Peay Building
University of Tennessee
Knoxville, TN 37996
E-mail: mikefinn@gmail.com

Michael Nash  

Neuropsychoanalytic theoretical and clinical work (Bazan 2013) gestures at the differences between how primary and secondary process thinkers experience their bodies in space. Not much is known about this,

NP8

however, though it seems that primary process thinkers may have more fluid body schemas than secondary process thinkers. That is, they may be more prone to subjective distortions of space. We first present a detailed analysis that demonstrates the individual differences between primary and secondary processing as measured by the GeoCat (Vanheule et al. 2011) on a novel task of implicit embodied distancing of self-to-objects (Embodied Distancing Task; Finn et al. 2013). These findings were examined from two perspectives: psychoanalytic theory on psychopathology and embodiment (Horowitz, Duff, and Stratton 1964; Muller and Tillman 2007) and existential-phenomenological perspectives on the self (Heidegger 1927; Merleau-Ponty 1945).

We sought to explore the notion that predominantly primary process thinkers experience their bodies, and thus the space around them, differently from how predominantly secondary process thinkers do. We examined the responses of participants on the Embodied Distance Task, a novel task of experiential distance distortions in peripersonal space.

Method

The 81 participants were 65.4% female, with an average age of 19.41 (SD = 4.57). Each participant was seated in front of a touchscreen monitor. The seat was adjusted in such a way that when relaxed in a seated posture, participants could touch the far edge of the screen without changing the position of their torso. This precluded the need for participants to move anything other than their dominant arm when responding to the task.

Stimuli were of three categories: simple geometric shapes, beverages, and household objects. The geometric shapes were designed in Adobe Photoshop CS5. The images of beverages and similarly sized household objects were found in various public domain image archives.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Negativity in Treatment

Nothing Good is Allowed to Stand: An Integrative View of the Negative Therapeutic Reaction. Edited by Léon Wurmser and Heidrun Jarass. New York: Routledge, 2013, xviii + 204 pp., $140.00 hardcover, $40.95 paperback.

Michael Shulman

Michael Shulman

117 North First Street, Suite 113
Ann Arbor, MI 48104
E-mail: drmichaelshulman@gmail.com

Michael Shulman   Faculty, Michigan Psychoanalytic Institute; adjunct faculty (psychiatry), University of Michigan.

Nothing Good Is Allowed to Stand serves at once as a partial epitaph, and a new beginning, for the concept of the negative therapeutic reaction. It is co-edited by Léon Wurmser, known for his intensive scholarship on some of the most difficult problems in clinical psychoanalysis: addiction, envy and pathological jealousy, pervasive shame, masochism, and the severe superego. Wurmser's co-editor, Heidrun Jarass, is an analyst in Nürnberg, Germany. In addition to serving as a bridge between the past and present status of a major psychoanalytic concept, Nothing Good Is Allowed to Stand spans North American, Latin American, and German perspectives on the negative therapeutic reaction, the last little known in the U.S. for reasons of psychoanalytic social history. (I would challenge any of my American psychoanalytic colleagues to name more than one contemporary German analyst.)

Nothing Good is partial epitaph, in that Freud's original formulation of the negative therapeutic reaction, in terms almost exclusively of a patient's response toward his own therapeutic gains due to guilt, is inevitably pushed to a corner as factors later appreciated in conceiving the origins of entrenched suffering and negativity in treatment come to the fore. In many ways, this is a volume interrogating marked patient negativity in analysis generally, rather than as a response to at least apparent improvement: it is really more often about “nothing good is allowed” than “nothing good is allowed to stand” (i.e., to be achieved and then undone) in an analysis. The editors contemplated maintaining a narrow definition of the negative therapeutic reaction, but in the end they, and the contributors to this volume, have concluded that such a definition is too slim to stand if it is to have clinical utility in today's world.

149

Psychoanalysis and Psychoanalytic Therapies by Jeremy Safran, a 2013 winner of the Gradiva Award for books from the National Association for the Advancement of Psychoanalysis, is a slim, gracefully written book that offers readers an integrative examination of the history, core concepts, and clinical applications of psychoanalysis. It is geared to the beginning reader but provides a rich synthesis of historical and contemporary ideas for the sophisticated reader as well. The book is part of the twenty-four-volume Theories of Psychotherapy Series from the American Psychological Association. Each volume in the series addresses a particular type of psychotherapy and has an accompanying DVD, sold separately, that demonstrates the therapy in action.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Transforming Trauma: The Relational Unconscious and “Chemistry” in the Treatment of a Paraplegic Patient

Claire Steinberger

Claire Steinberger

16 West 61th Street
New York, N Y 10011
E-mail: ctein3981@aol.com

Claire Steinberger  

trauma, working through, psychoanalysis, relational chemistry, relational unconscious

Therapeutic action with a traumatized paraplegic patient highlights the evocative—and transformative—influence of the relational unconscious. The patient's triumphant resolution suggests that formative bipersonal dynamics (including transference, countertransference, and mutual projective identifications) create an ongoing intersubjective enactment and relational chemistry pivotal to psychic shift. A broad systems perspective highlights contextual communication and the interweaving of the analyst's etiological contributions and subjective experience of trauma. Ultimately, an unconscious, co-created dynamic challenges traumatic fixations, supporting a reintegration of narcissistic, gender, and erotic representations and ego capacities. An eclectic perspective that illuminates the analyst's role-receptivity encompasses classical, object relations, relational, systems, and self psychological paradigms.

David Stevens  

By an artful weaving of scholarship, diverse video clips, and a realtime projected Twitter1 feed that provided ongoing audience reactions to the panel's unfolding, Phillip Freeman, with the help of fellow panelists Stephen Hartman, Glen Gabbard, and Vera Camden created a highly engaging educational, experiential, and consciousness-raising experience about that complex and highly diverse aggregate of phenomena subsumed under the term virtual reality. Given the panel's great breadth, the number of intriguing ideas and observations that were introduced, and the clinical relevance of the papers, this report can present but a few of the many significant ideas that were conveyed.

Following her introduction of the panelists, moderator Vera Camden framed the afternoon's discussion by noting the extraordinary speed with which technological advances have created a new world, the world of cyberspace, a world that has significant effects on the nature of human subjectivity. Camden referenced Katherine Hayles's recent work on the “posthuman” (2012)

1Phillip Freeman taught a dozen analysts in the audience to tweet during the panel, and their live tweets, and those of some participants as far away as California, were projected at the front of the room so that the audience could read them in real time during the panel discussions.

Panel held at the Winter Meeting of the American Psychoanaltytic Association, New York, January 18, 2014. Panelists: Vera Camden (chair), Phillip Freeman, Glen O. Gabbard, Stephen Hartman.

responseSet.title: Mourning, Identity, Creativity
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mourning, Identity, Creativity

Tehela Nimroody

Tehela Nimroody

250 East 65th Street, 11G
New York, NY 10065
E-mail: tnimroody@att.net

Tehela Nimroody  

In “Mourning and Melancholia” Freud (1917) delineated a normative path for healthy mourning, one in which the bereaved, with the help of repeated reality testing, severs emotional attachments to the lost one and reinvests the free libido in a new object. Much debate regarding the validity of these ideas has ensued since their publication and has taken various forms. In this panel discussion on mourning, identity, and creativity, the panelists used their personal experiences of grief to inform their theories and addressed various questions such as the relinquishment of ties to the lost one, internalization, identification, special cases of loss such as genocide, and the relation between mourning and creativity.

The discussion was opened by the chair, Adele Tutter. She observed that while the literature on attachment and intimacy is quite abundant, there is a relative paucity of literature on the mourning and loss of loved ones, perhaps precisely because the process of mourning is so painful. In losing a loved one, we lose a part of ourselves. After mentioning some philosophical and aesthetic perspectives on the theme of loss, and a reference to the national tragedy of 9/11 as a model of loss and mourning, Tutter touched on Melanie Klein's idea that mourning is the loss not only of the present object but of the earliest internal objects as well, which in the process of mourning are lost yet again. Finally, she spoke about the association between creativity and mourning, and postulated that while mourning often stimulates creativity, creativity may also facilitate mourning.

Otto Kernberg was the first of the panelists to speak. The purpose of his presentation, he made clear, was to describe the process of mourning under ideal conditions in which ambivalence is minimal, as opposed to the pathological mourning processes he has addressed in other papers. He described his findings upon doing follow-up interviews with a group of widows and widowers he had interviewed five years earlier.

The New Dictionary of Kleinian Thought. By Elizabeth Bott Spillius, Jane Milton, Penelope Garvey, Cyril Couve, and Deborah Steiner. New York: Routledge, 2011, xiv + 557 pp., $150.00 hardcover, $47.95 paperback.

Robert Galatzer-Levy

Robert Galatzer-Levy

122 South Michigan Avenue, Suite 1407
Chicago, IL 60603
E-mail: gala@uchicago.edu

Robert Galatzer-Levy   Training, Supervising, and Child and Adolescent Supervising Analyst, Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis; Clinical Professor, Department of Psychiatry and Behavioral Neuroscience, University of Chicago.

A wonderful panel at the 2013 Prague Congress of the IPA discussed the Kleinian concept of reparation. Led by a North American analyst, the panel engaged its international audience through its amazing clarity and close tracking of the connection between clinical data and theory. After the meeting, conversations continued, enthusiastically exploring aspects of the presentations.

A brief twenty years ago, at a similar panel, a North American analyst presented a patient for discussion by a modern Kleinian from London. In her initial discussion the Kleinian referred frequently to psychotic elements in the analysand's personality. The ensuing conversation centered around whether the analysand was psychotic from the viewpoint of descriptive psychiatry and analyzability. In an ever more frustrating debate that gave new meaning to the phrase “confusion of tongues,” and with hardly less affect than the child-adult sexual interaction to which Ferenczi (1949) was referring might generate, the presenter and members of the audience tried to defend the analysand's claim to sanity, while the discussant and others in the audience insisted on the richer understanding of the clinical material afforded by the Kleinian viewpoint. Informal talk after the meeting continued the debate—who was crazier, the patient or the Kleinian?

Another thirty years or so earlier, leading American analysts had dismissed Kleinian thought, contrasting their “ego psychology” with Kleinian “id mythology” (Rapaport and Gill 1958). Indeed, in reading Klein it is often difficult to tell whether she is referring to the analysand's conscious or unconscious picture of his own mental functioning or to the analyst's ideas about that mental life.

[[RunningHead]]

The Possible Profession: The Analytic Process of Change. By Theodore J. Jacobs. New York: Routledge, 2013, viii + 326 pp., $44.95 paperback.

Richard Honig

Richard Honig

316 Great Barrington Road
Housatonic, MA 01236
E-mail: rhonig@honigmd.com

Richard Honig   Training and Supervising Analyst, Cincinnati Psychoanalytic Institute and Berkshire Psychoanalytic Institute; Assistant Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, University of Cincinnati College of Medicine.

To read The Possible Profession: The Analytic Process of Change is to take a master's class in the art of psychoanalysis taught by a consummate clinician and theorist in matters of therapeutic action and technique. Jacobs also displays his skills as an accomplished writer and storyteller able to create an entertaining, at times compelling reading experience that powerfully resonates with the communicative matters at the heart of his work. The clinical vignettes with which this volume is replete are both exquisitely personal and at the same time scholarly as he embraces the central debates and controversies of the psychoanalytic times in which he has lived.

In his 1991 book, The Use of the Self: Countertransference and Communication in the Analytic Situation, Jacobs charted the territory that has continued to occupy his professional thought and writing for these succeeding two decades. That book collected in one volume the groundbreaking contributions he had published between 1973 and 1990, works that opened new vistas in the understanding of therapeutic communication in clinical psychoanalysis. The volume now at hand traces the rich elaboration of those seminal ideas, which to a significant degree have been on the leading edge of contemporary psychoanalytic dialogue. A classically trained analyst in the ego psychological tradition, Jacobs has challenged many tenets once sacrosanct, always with an eye to the therapeutic aims of the analytic endeavor. He has been a pioneer in the study of the intersection of the intra- and intersubjective dimensions of the psychoanalytic encounter, while at the same time asking us to consider whether the current emphasis on interaction and intersubjectivity is taking us away from the heart and core of analysis: “Clothed in modern, sophisticated terms, does this focus actually disguise and rationalize the basic tendency in all of us—the ever-present wish to avoid both the anxiety, the pain, and the disruption that inevitably accompany immersion in the unconscious of another and the arousal within us of the dark forces of aggression and sexuality that accompany such immersion” (p. 214).

Extending Psychoanalysis

Enemies on the Couch: A Psychopolitical Journey through War and Peace. By Vamik D. Volkan. Durham, NC: Pitchstone Publishing, 2013, xxx + 504 pp., $29.95.

Peter Loewenberg

Peter Loewenberg

449 Levering Avenue
Los Angeles, CA 90024-1909
E-mail: peterl@ucla.edu

Peter Loewenberg   Training and Supervising Analyst and former Dean, New Center for Psychoanalysis, Los Angeles; Professor of History and Political Psychology, Emeritus, UCLA.

This is an intensely personal book full of first-person illustrative vignettes with the qualities of good autobiography. Volkan is a Turkish Cypriot, a son of Cyprus, who was born and received his childhood socialization and schooling through high school there. He is a founding member and sometime president of the International Society of Political Psychology (ISPP). At the University of Virginia, where he is now Professor Emeritus of Psychiatry, he founded the Center for the Study of Mind and Human Interaction and its journal, Mind and Human Interaction. In 1999 he was the Sigmund Freud Visiting Scholar in Vienna.

Volkan is especially strong on the minor group differences in Cyprus, the psychological turf he knows most intimately. Cyprus, a Greco-Turkish island, lies at the Eastern Mediterranean intersection of Europe, Asia, and North Africa, as does Volkan's work. Its situation is the strategic reason Disraeli acquired the island for Britain in 1878. Volkan's observations are intimate, filled with childhood and family memories, ful1 of rich in-depth observations of symbolic practices like bird collecting: “The birds in cages represented the externalized self-representations, mental doubles, of the Cypriot Turks. As long as the birds produced new birds, sang and survived, the Cypriot Turks could hope for survival” (p. 145). He presents vignettes of how child-rearing practices (e.g., circumcision in latency) and family structure, including multiple mothering, influence perceptions of the world and of enemies. As Volkan poignantly observes, the cultural and social similarities, including dress, among Greek and Turkish Cypriots are much greater than the superficial differences that have been inflamed by projection, war, trauma, and hatred. He is gifted and understands his own subjectivity.

757

[[RunningHead]]

Coming to Terms with Intersubjectivity: Keeping Language in Mind

Bonnie Litowitz

Bonnie Litowitz

E-mail: bonlit@gmail.com

Bonnie Litowitz  

complementarity, deception, intersubjectivity, joint reference, language, mediation, negation, semiotics

Recent reports of clinical experiences, from multiple theoretical perspectives using different terminologies, converge on the topic of the origin and nature of intersubjectivity as it emerges in clinical interactions. Intersubjectivity is explored from the point of view of language and communication, and the distinctive properties of language that create a shared intrapsychic world, on which intersubjectivity subtends, are discussed. This viewpoint can explain behavior and pathologies that arise from the struggle to maintain one's personal subjectivity in an intersubjective world. Understanding the role and functions of language may also illuminate the therapeutic benefits of talk-based therapies.

[[RunningHead]]

Exploring A Complex Systems Map of Gender: Commentary on Saketopoulou

Robert Galatzer-Levy

Robert Galatzer-Levy

122 South Michigan Avenue
Chicago, IL 60603
E-mail: gala@uchicago.edu

Robert Galatzer-Levy  

Avgi Saketopoulou provides a stunning integration of theory, empirical studies, and clinical work. She continues the process by which a broadening view of transsexual people both enriches our understanding of gender and helps us assist them with the challenges they commonly face. It is fitting that the paper won the Roughton Award, not only because of its excellence, but because, like the existence of that award itself, the paper marks a sea change in psychoanalytic outlook and practice from a time when analysts acted as part of the oppressive dominant community by pathologizing sexual and gender variation to a time when analysts have largely recognized that these variations represent life ways deserving of respect and support, while at the same time maintaining a commitment to an understanding of their multiple significance for people.

In late-nineteenth-century Europe and the United States, the possibility of separating sexual activity from reproduction, combined with the relaxation of antisexual moral attitudes and the recognition that sexual frustration contributes significantly to psychological distress, converged to create an intellectual and medical environment that invited exploration of the complexities of gender and sex. In the latter part of the nineteenth century Kraft-Ebing (1886) conceptualized the variations he found as forms of pathology, thereby moving discourse about sexuality away from morality and law and toward medicine. He began a catalog of the range of human sexuality and gender.

Clinical Professor of Psychiatry and Behavioral Neurosciences, University of Chicago; Training, Supervising, and Child and Adolescent Supervising Analyst, Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis.

[[RunningHead]]

What Does “Brief” Mean? A Theoretical Critique of the Concept of Brief Therapy from a Psychoanalytic Viewpoint

Paolo Migone

Paolo Migone

Via Palestro, 14
43123 Parma PR
ITALY
E-mail: migone@unipr.it

Paolo Migone  

brief psychotherapy, short-term dynamic psychotherapy, termination, critique, experiential factor, theory of psychoanalytic technique, parameters of technique, psychoanalytic psychotherapy

The concept of “brief therapy” contains internal contradictions. The techniques suggested by brief therapists are the same techniques that have been discussed historically in debates on theory of technique, both within and without psychoanalysis (e.g., the experiential factor at the center of the Freud-Ferenczi confrontation is also an important aspect of Gestalt therapy). A time limit is the only operational criterion that allows a rigorous definition of brief therapy; without this criterion it is impossible to discriminate between “brief” and simply “good” therapies (i.e., those in which patients are successfully treated in a short time). An important question is why, with a given patient, a therapist should decide, a priori, to terminate a treatment within a set time. Two clinical examples are presented, illustrating the usefulness of Eissler's concept of parameters as a heuristic framework to identify the potentially defensive nature of both “brief” and “long-term” therapy.

[[RunningHead]]

A Clinical Plenary: Introduction

Humphrey Morris

Humphrey Morris

8 Irving Terrace
Cambridge, MA 02138-3011
E-mail: humphreymorris@gmail.com

Humphrey Morris  

A new plenary format, inaugurated by Alfred Margulies, Shelley Orgel, and Warren Poland, was introduced at the June 2014 meetings of the American Psychoanalytic Association in Chicago: a clinical presentation with commentaries by discussants. Margulies gave his account of an analysis, “After the Storm: Living and Dying in Psychoanalysis,” in two parts corresponding to two periods of intense engagement, years apart, between analyst and analysand: “The Storm: Flirting with Death” and “Return.” After each part, Orgel and Poland responded, making back-and-forth exchange organic to the program. A general discussion with the audience concluded the three-hour session.

Margulies's plenary, as he delivered it, was remarkable for the directness and simplicity of its engagement with the audience. JAPA has tried to preserve that quality of engagement here by presenting the talk as it was given, including pauses intended by Margulies to convey rhythmic shifts in the clinical exchange. At the same time, publication will allow readers to appreciate the complexity of narrative structure and theoretical synthesis in this text. Its accessibility as an oral presentation, a triumph of experiential storytelling, belied its conceptual depth, for Margulies did not interrupt the flow of his story to provide citations or explanations. Now that the talk reappears here as a text to be read and reread, I will take the opportunity in this introduction to point to some of the distinct theoretical commitments and clinical perspectives it weaves together. A good place to start is by noting that the two discussants, in their own published work, offer views of what it is to be an analyst that contrapuntally frame what Margulies is both saying and doing in his plenary.

Poland gave the American Psychoanalytic Association's plenary address in May 1998 in Toronto. His title was “The Analyst's Witnessing

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud's Jewish Identity and Psychoanalysis as a Science

Arnold Richards

Arnold Richards

200 East 89th Street
New York, NY 10128
E-mail: arniedr15@gmail.com

Arnold Richards  

Freud, psychoanalysis, Jewish identity

Ludwik Fleck, the Polish philosopher of science, maintained that scientific discovery is influenced by social, political, historical, psychological, and personal factors. The determinants of Freud's Jewish identity are examined from this Fleckian perspective, as is the impact of that complex identity on his creation of psychoanalysis as a science. Three strands contributing to his Jewish identity are identified and explored: his commitment to the ideal of Bildung, the anti-Semitism of the times, and his “godlessness.” Finally, the question is addressed of what it means that psychoanalysis was founded by a Jew. For Freud, psychoanalysis was a kind of liberation philosophy, an attempt to break free of his ethnic and religious inheritance. Yet it represented at the same time his ineradicable relationship with that inheritance. It encapsulated both the ambivalence of his Jewish identity and the creativity of his efforts to resolve it.

Transformation of Personality: Deformation and Reformation in Confronting Death

Breaking Bad. Television series created by Vince Gilligan. American Movie Channel, seasons 1-5 (2008-2013).The Duel. By Anton Chekhov. Translated by Margarita Shalina. Brooklyn: Melville House, 2011, 144 pp., $11.00 paperback.

Ilany Kogan

Ilany Kogan

2 Mohaliver Street
Rehovot
ISRAEL
E-mail: ilanyk@yahoo.com

Ilany Kogan   Training Analyst, Israeli Psychoanalytic Society.

In this essay I will explore personality transformation in the confrontation with death. This transformation may occur in consequence of realizing the finality of life, an inability to mourn, or the impact of this confrontation on the superego. I will examine the ensuing de- or reformation the superego may undergo under the threat of death and the effect of these processes on the whole personality.

To illustrate the process of superego deformation under the threat of death, I will analyze the personality of Walter White, protagonist of the critically acclaimed American television drama series Breaking Bad (2008-2013). I will then analyze the reformation of the superego when confronted with a near-death experience in the story of Ivan Andreich Laevsky, protagonist of Anton Chekhov's novella The Duel (1891), a screen adaptation of which appeared in 2010.1

I will compare and contrast how each of these protagonists reacts to the confrontation with death. I will explore their inability to mourn, their fear of death, and their use of manic defenses to counter their depressive anxiety, as well as the phenomenon of character growth in extreme personal crises.

1Screenplay by Mary Bing, directed by Dover Kosashvili (http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anton_Chekhov's_The_Duel).719

[[RunningHead]]

Encountering Erich Fromm

The Lives of Erich Fromm: Love's Prophet. By Lawrence J. Friedman. New York: Columbia University Press, 2013, liv + 410 pp., $29.95.

Adrienne Harris

Adrienne Harris

80 University Place, 5th floor
New York, NY 10003
E-mail: adrienneharris@gmail.com

Adrienne Harris  

The Lives of Erich Fromm, Lawrence J. Friedman's new biography of Erich Fromm, has rightly been seen, in the nonanalytic circles in which it has been reviewed, as an evenhanded, rich treatment of Fromm in his professional, intellectual, political, and sociocultural contexts. Lives indeed. In his acknowledgments, Friedman outlines a great number of extensive debts he feels he owes to friends and colleagues for help in many specific areas in preparing what he calls the hardest book he has had to write. I learned an enormous amount about Fromm in this detailed and meticulous book, but I remain puzzled about the variable and shifting places of Fromm in the interlocking yet distinct worlds he inhabited. It is both to explore this puzzle and to revive interest in some important controversies in psychoanalysis that I have undertaken this essay.

Erich Fromm was born in 1900 in Frankfurt and died in Muralto, Switzerland, in 1980. He grew up into a scene of intellectual ferment and deep engagement, in respect to Jewish studies, Marxism, and psychoanalysis. He was in contact with the leadership in psychoanalysis, undertook analysis with Hanns Sachs after a disastrous experience with Frieda Reichmann that ended in a boundary violation. He had links to Georg Groddeck and therefore to the work of Ferenczi in Europe and later, in a growing tilt toward an interpersonal, sociocultural psychoanalytic tradition, he established strong connections to Sullivan, Horney, and Thompson upon immigrating to the United States in 1934.

He was a central figure and actually the savior of the Frankfurt Institute, which, under threat in Europe, relocated to America under Fromm's care and management.

The Politics of Psychoanalytic Lexicography

Elizabeth Auchincloss and Eslee Samberg

Eslee Samberg

15 West 81st Street
New York, NY 10024-6022
E-mail: elauchin@med.cornell.edu

165 West End Avenue, Suite 1M
New York, NY 10023-5504 E-mail: esamberg@gmail.com

Eslee Samberg   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Weill Cornell Medical College; Training and Supervising Analyst, New York Psychoanalytic Institute.

I would rather have trusted the refinement of our language … to the judgment of women, than of illiterate court fops, half-witted poets and University boys.

—Jonathan Swift, 1712

In the last week of February 2012, state administrators across France received a memo from Prime Minister François Fillon informing them that it was no longer acceptable to use the word “mademoiselle” in any official form or registry. Fillon's order came after an advocacy campaign of many months on the part of French feminist organizations, who argued that use of the word reflects distinctions reserved only for women, and that official forms include no word for unmarried men or “young male virgins.” With a stroke of the pen a familiar word that entered the French lexicon in the late seventeenth century and began its pubic career in the civil code of Napoleon I was banished from official use (Sayare 2012). The fact is, word use is a political matter.

In June 2004 the American Psychoanalytic Association asked us to assume the positions of co-editors-in-chief of a revised version of Psychoanalytic Terms and Concepts, the organization's lexicon/dictionary/encyclopedia (Auchincloss and Samberg 2012). The moment we accepted this assignment, we became involved not only in a lexicographical undertaking, but a political undertaking, too. In this essay we will discuss not only how the book was constructed, but also some of the challenges we have encountered in the politics of psychoanalytic lexicography.

We began our discussion of the subject with an example of “word politics” from France. Closer to home, on this side of the Atlantic, arguments at the interface between politics and lexicography also abound. If you don't believe that words have political significance, try going into a

A version of this essay has been presented at the Washington-Baltimore Psychoanalytic Institute, the University of New Mexico Department of Psychiatry, Weill Cornell Medical College Department of Psychiatry, the Boston Psychoanalytic Institute, the American Psychoanalytic Association, and the New York Psychoanalytic Institute.

[[RunningHead]]

Drawing Trauma: The Therapeutic Potential of Witnessing the Child's Visual Testimony of War

Lisa Farley and Aparna Tarc

Aparna Tarc

4700 Keele Street
Toronto, Ontario, M3J 1P3
CANADA
E-mail: lfarley@edu.yorku,ca

4700 Keele Street
Toronto, Ontario, M3J 1P3
CANADA

Aparna Tarc  

Countertransference plays an often neglected role in witnessing children's testimony of war and trauma. A dual notion of countertransference, based on the work of Winnicott and Klein, is offered that involves both internal conflict related to early life experience and socially mediated notions of childhood, war, and trauma circulating in a given time and place. A drawing by a thirteen-year-old boy living in the refugee camps in Darfur is used to show how countertransference affects our interpretation of the image, even while its symbolization in language establishes the conditions for a potentially therapeutic response. It is argued that a psychoanalytic reading can supplement the “legal-conscious terminology” in which the Darfur archive has been predominantly framed (Felman 2002, p. 5). This expanded view of witnessing involves reading the child's testimony both for the history of violence it conveys and for the social and emotional histories it calls up in the witness as the ground and possibility of justice.

Journal Watch

F. Leichsenring, S. Salzer, M.E. Beutel, S. Herpertz, W. Hiller, J. Hoyer, J. Husing, P. Joraschky, B. Nolting, K. Poehlmann, V. Ritter, U. Stangier, B. Strauss, S. Tefikow, T. Teismann, U. Willutzki, J. Wiltink, & E. Leibing (2014). Long-term outcome of psychodynamic therapy and cognitive-behavioral therapy in social anxiety disorder.American Journal of Psychiatry171:1074-1082.

Sargam Jain

Sargam Jain

Sargam Jain  

The authors of this study chose to observe, at five sites throughout Germany, the efficacy of psychodynamic psychotherapy as a treatment for people diagnosed with social anxiety disorder as compared to their receiving cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) or no treatment at all. The study was performed between 2007 and 2009; results of the study immediately after completion of treatment were first published last year (Leichsenring et al. 2013). This 2014 paper examines the long-term outcomes of the study following completion of treatment at 6, 12, and 24 months.

An interesting aspect of this study is the attempt to manualize and define psychodynamic psychotherapy. CBT was administered using Clark and Wells's manualized treatment for social phobia (Clark and Wells 1995; Wells 1997). Aware that no manualized form of psychodynamic psychotherapy exists, the researchers defined the therapy by adapting Luborsky's manualized supportive-expressive therapy for social phobia (1984), a treatment not previously studied. Establishing a secure helping alliance and elaborating a core conflictual relationship theme related to the symptoms of anxiety are central features of this treatment. Both treatments were “dosed” in twenty-five individual sessions of fifty minutes each. Because CBT allows for double sessions, those in the psychodynamic arm of the study were allowed twice-weekly sessions during the middle part of the treatment (sessions 7 to 16); the authors note that this was done to match CBT in terms of session minutes, and not necessarily to reflect the impact of session frequency on transference.

In all, 1,450 patients were screened, 495 of whom met the study's inclusion criteria.

Pamela Cooper-White

Pamela Cooper-White

701 South Columbia Drive
Decatur, GA 30030
E-mail: cooperwhitep@ctsnet.edu

Pamela Cooper-White   Ben G. & Nancye Clapp Gautier Professor of Pastoral Theology, Care, and Counseling, Columbia Theological Seminary, Decatur, GA; 2013-14 Fulbright-Freud Scholar of Psychoanalysis, Vienna.

As is well known, religion, and in particular the figure of Moses, haunted Freud throughout his life. Freud's complex, multivalent relationship to his Jewish heritage has caused scholars of psychology and religion to “put Freud on the couch” many times to discern the psychological causes of his repudiation of the “oceanic feeling”—the psychological experience of religion, a term coined by Romain Rolland—coupled with his adamant identification with the Jewish people as a race. From the early 1900s Freud classified religious rituals and practices as akin to obsessional symptoms, and religion as a bearer of severe moralism and a source of pathogenic repression. Expanding on the subject of religion in his later years, especially after the solidification of his oedipal theory, Freud increasingly focused in his cultural writings on religion as a psychic defense against the harsh demands of reality, an expression of an infantile wish fulfillment for a soothing escape from Ananke (necessity). Religion (according to Freud's speculative reading of prehistory as primal parricide and reparation) and its resulting patriarchal law (expanded via linguistics by Jacques Lacan) were the primal building blocks of civilization. Freud intentionally avoided proposing any ethical or moral prescriptions for behavior, and even refused to acknowledge any distinctive weltanschauung within psychoanalysis—other than adherence to the scientific method and allegiance to seeking the “truth,” however unpopular or uncomfortable.

On Paolo Migone's “What Does ‘Brief’ Mean?”

Allan Abbass , Peter Lilliengren and Joel Town

Joel Town

Halifax, NS
CANADA
E-mail: allan.abbass@dal.ca

SWEDEN

Halifax, NS
CANADA

Joel Town  

We are writing regarding Paolo Migone's “What Does ‘Brief’ Mean?” (JAPA 624), in which he discusses various issues related to brief therapy from the perspective of psychoanalysis. We found the paper interesting and concur with many of his views. Our own clinical and research focus is in the area of Habib Davanloo's model, intensive short-term dynamic psychotherapy (ISTDP). From this perspective and with an up-to-date understanding of ISTDP, we are writing to clarify certain aspects of the model.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Both Given and Made: Commentary on Saketopoulou

Muriel Dimen

Muriel Dimen

3 East 10th Street, Suite 1B1
New York, NY 10003
E-mail: murieldimen@nyu.edu

Muriel Dimen  

Once upon a time, sex and gender were a team. Gender aligned with natal sex, and both aligned with desire (heterosexual, to be sure): male genitalia rendered you masculine and desirous of women; female genitalia made you feminine and inclined you toward men. Nowadays everything is up for grabs. No longer do genitals and gender predict desire: same-sex and same-gender love, like changes in desire across lifetimes, are deemed normal. Our grasp of the shifting relations among the categories once grouped under the rubric of “psychosexuality” is much more nuanced. Consider Saketopoulou's subtle assessment of her patient's possible futures: “Jenny might be a transgender girl rather than a proto-gay boy” (p. 788).

It was Freud (1905), of course, who first opened this Pandora's box. As he contended, drive, aim, and object are linked only loosely. Now, however, the sexual stays are loosed even further. We are challenged to consider that, since genitalia and gender identity are themselves but soldered together, any mismatch between them could cause sufficient psychic pain that surgical interventions might be just what the patient, and therefore the doctor, ordered. I won't opine on what Freud would have said about sexual reassignment or gender confirmation surgery, although at the close of “Psychogenesis of a Case of Homosexuality in a Woman” (1920, p. 172) he does wonder about it, albeit more to control his enigmatic and frustrating patient and less to serve her desire (Harris 1991).

That gender dysphoria may now be assuaged by surgical modification obliges psychoanalysis to reconstrue the body.

Adjunct Clinical Professor of Psychology, NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis; Professor Emerita of Anthropology, Lehman College (CUNY); Fellow, New York Institute for the Humanities, New York University.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Research

Resilience: The Science of Mastering Life's Greatest Challenges. By Steven M. Southwick and Dennis S. Charney. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2012, viii + 230 pp., $23.99 paperback.

Anne Adelman

Anne Adelman

5480 Wisconsin Avenue, Suite 215A
Chevy Chase, MD 20815
E-mail: adelmanaj@comcast.net

Anne Adelman   Member, Contemporary Freudian Society; faculty, New Directions Program, Washington Center for Psychoanalysis.

These days you'll likely find me, an inveterate right-hander, brushing my teeth in the bathroom with my left hand. Or else I might be busy folding my laundry in the dark, by touch, using only my fingertips to distinguish among the familiar, warmly scented clean clothes. Standing in line at the grocery store, I'm no longer idly perusing the headlines of the tabloids—instead I'm memorizing the items in the grocery cart ahead of me, and their prices, and then randomly matching them to the shopper's clothing, which goes something like this: “Quart of fat-free milk, $2.99, white shirt.” Driving home, I'm reciting the list from memory. And at yoga, I'm no longer rolling up my mat and rushing out to see a patient while others are in repose. Instead, I'm reclaiming those three minutes for meditation and reflection, allowing myself the moments to breathe deeply, in through the nose, out through the mouth.

Since I finished reading Steven Southwick and Dennis Charney's Resilience, I've been working on my physical and mental fitness, challenging my cognitive and motor dexterity, practicing mindfulness and trying to look on the bright side of things. In other words, I'm working on building my resilience. We may wonder whether resilience can be learned. Southwick and Charney believe it can. In this book they show us how to do it. The authors offer evidence that resilience can be taught and honed through rigorous training, just as one can train to strengthen the muscles of the body or acquire new technical skills. The wide array of approaches they describe range from learning adaptive responses to fear, to increasing cognitive and emotional flexibility, to developing a fitness plan for the brain. If you stay with it and practice in a sustained way, Southwick and Charney promise, resilience factors will increase over time and build on one another, leading to a fuller and richer existence.
