[[RunningHead]]

Psychic Reality in Context: Perspectives on Psychoanalysis, Personal History, and Trauma. By Marion M. Oliner. London: Karnac Books, 2012, l + 142 pp., $37.95 paperback.

Anna Balas

Anna Balas

1235 Park Avenue, #1B
New York, NY 10128
E-mail: annabalasmd@gmail.com

Anna Balas   Associate Professor of Psychiatry, New York Presbyterian Hospital-Cornell Medical Center, Payne Whitney Division; faculty, New York Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; member, Center for Advanced Psychoanalytic Studies.

Two things struck me most in reading Psychic Reality in Context: Perspectives on Psychoanalysis, Personal History, and Trauma: first, Marion Oliner's personal resiliency; second, the soundness of her theoretical and clinical thinking integrated with her own experience. This slim volume qualifies as a primer on trauma, discussing as it does the central controversies in the field. Oliner has a very specific point of view worthy of close attention.

Oliner, born in Germany before World War II, suffered a series of dislocations and indignities culminating in the loss of her immediate family. She devotes her second prologue to a personal narrative titled “Excuse Me for Having Been Born: The Fate of a German Jew during the Second World War.” Recounting her personal history before and during the war, she writes with the objective eye of a psychoanalyst and with characteristic candor, recollecting how she coped and protected herself emotionally as she underwent terrifying and humiliating experiences. For instance, when marching with a convoy of refugee children on the way to Switzerland, the twelve-year-old Marion slipped and fell into some bushes. She overheard the woman leader of the group tell her husband to leave her behind. He went back to rescue her anyway, and she made it to safety. Oliner's description conveys both her immense suffering and the appalling injustice of her circumstances.

In her quest to master her childhood losses, Oliner has been drawn to the subject of trauma, in both theory and clinical practice. On the theoretical side, she describes the prototypical traumatic situation as one in which the demands of reality temporarily override operation of the pleasure principle. A number of factors determine the severity of a person's reaction to a potentially traumatic situation: level of maturity at the time of the occurrence; the emotional intensity of the events; whether the situation needs to be kept secret or whether there are corroborating witnesses. Another factor enters when a current situation is perceived as “perceptually identical” with a past event. That similarity can trigger severe symptoms because external and internal reality collide, making the person feel overwhelmed.

Psychoanalytic Modalities

Manual of Panic Focused Psychodynamic Psychotherapy—Extended Range. By Frederic N. Busch, Barbara L. Milrod, Meriamne B. Singer, and Andrew C. Aronson. New York: Routledge, 2012, 215 pp., $40.95.

Nicole Nehrig and Philip Wong

Philip Wong

E-mail: njnehrig@gmail.com

Brooklyn, NY 11201
E-mail: philip.wong@liu.edu

Philip Wong   Professor of Psychology and Director, Ph.D. Program in Clinical Psychology, Department of Psychology, Long Island University.

The Manual of Panic Focused Psychodynamic Psychotherapy—eXtended Range (PFPP-XR1) expands panic focused psychodynamic psychotherapy (PFPP), originally developed in 1997 and empirically validated for the treatment of panic disorder, to treat other anxiety disorders such as agoraphobia, generalized anxiety disorder, social anxiety, obsessive-compulsive disorder, and posttraumatic stress disorder. The treatment as developed here is the first psychoanalytically based psychotherapy to have demonstrated efficacy as a sole treatment modality for DSM-IV Axis I Anxiety Disorders. It makes a valuable contribution to the effort within the psychoanalytic community to remain competitive in a field whose trajectory indicates a continuing shift toward manualized, empirically supported short-term therapies. But what does this movement mean for practitioners of psychodynamic psychotherapy, who have generally appreciated, if not cherished, a relatively unstructured approach? Psychoanalytic theory demands use of the therapist as an instrument of change (e.g., through the exploration of transference and countertransference); the therapist must respond authentically and spontaneously to such material as it arises. Many practitioners would feel constrained by a therapeutic model that imposes too much structure on therapy sessions. This balance between structure and freedom has heretofore marked one of the primary tensions between the psychodynamic and CBT models.

1The abbreviation calls to mind pharmaceutical “eXtended Release” medications.943

Günther Klug  

Within the framework of our process-outcome study investigating the relationship between emotional processes and therapy outcome in three forms of long-term therapy, this poster focuses on the training and application of the Experiencing Scale (EXP; Klein et al. 1969, 1986). This research instrument will be applied in order to capture emotional processing in psychoanalytic, psychodynamic, and cognitive-behavioral therapies as investigated in the Munich Psychotherapy Study.1 The investigation of emotional processing among psychotherapy process research is gaining increasing relevance (see, e.g., Fonagy et al. 2002; Greenberg 2002)

1The Munich Psychotherapy Study (MPS) is a process-outcome study that compared psychoanalytic, psychodynamic, and cognitive-behavioral therapy (Huber et al. 2012a,b, 2013).NP4

[[RunningHead]]

JAPA New Author Prize

JAPA's New Author Prize recognizes significant contributions to the journal by new authors. The prize is awarded for the best published manuscript by a new author, and includes a $500 honorarium that is presented at the Winter Meetings of the American Psychoanalytic Association.

A new author is considered to be someone who has not previously published in JAPA or any other major psychoanalytic journal and whose psychoanalytic career is in its early stages. The editor-in-chief has established a mentoring/editorial group to review submissions by new authors and assist in developing manuscripts for publication in JAPA.

Manuscripts must be submitted via the usual JAPA submission process using the online system accessed at http://mc.manuscriptcentral.com/japa. At the time of submission, an author may indicate in the cover letter that the manuscript is to be considered for the New Author Prize, and may ask that the paper be directed to the mentoring/editorial group for assistance in manuscript development. Final decision about publication remains with the editorial board and the editor-in-chief, as does the decision about selecting the prize-winning paper.

Richard Waugaman   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry and Faculty Expert on Shakespeare for Media Contacts, Georgetown University; Training and Supervising Analyst Emeritus, Washington Psychoanalytic Institute.

Richard Bachman is a well-known author of several successful novels. Inexplicably, conspiracy theorists invented the improbable scenario that another person secretly wrote his books. This would mean that numerous people are keeping silent about the true author. So no rational person could believe such a preposterous idea.

Actually, as some of you know, several years after the first Richard Bachman novel appeared,4 a perceptive bookstore clerk found evidence linking these novels to the best-selling author Stephen King, and King then confessed that he did write them. This is just one of the countless, endlessly fascinating stories of pseudonymous authorship that are receiving more attention during the past decade.

It is true that William Shakspere of Stratford was once the “most plausible contender” (p. 30) for authorship of the Shakespearean canon. The name “William Shakespeare” (often hyphenated) appeared on some early editions of the canonical plays and poems. Ben Jonson listed “William Shake-Speare” (as he spelled it) as an actor in the first performance of Jonson's play Sejanus. And Jonson's poem in the 1623 First Folio of collected plays is widely thought to prove Shakspere's authorship. Diligent research in the early nineteenth century uncovered many letters by Shakspere to his patron, the Earl of Southampton. But the plausibility of Shakspere's authorship was strongest before these letters were exposed as forgeries5; before scholars (e.g., North 2003; Starner and Traister 2011) began recognizing the frequency and complexity of anonymous authorship in Elizabethan literature (e.g., hyphenated authors' names often signaled that they were pen names); and before solid research undermined every other foundation of the theory that Shakspere of Stratford wrote the works. Scholars such as David Riggs (1989), himself a “Stratfordian,” have shown convincingly that Ben Jonson was a master of ambiguity. The Shakespearean scholar George Steevens

Although focused on the last two years of Freud's life, The Death of Sigmund Freud begins decades earlier, juxtaposing two inhabitants of Vienna in 1909: Freud at fifty-three, well on in developing psychoanalysis, with an already considerable following; and Adolf Hitler in his twenties, living abstemiously, doing odd jobs, scavenging, nursing grudges against art schools that judged him untalented, and giving impromptu, frenzied, at times ridiculed talks in the men's shelter in which he lived—about corrupt establishments, hateful Jews and Communists, and Germany's exalted destiny. The story then skips to 1938-1939 and remains there—when, having been cited for heroism in World War I, politically energized thereafter, and in time triumphant, Hitler, as Chancellor of Germany and at the height of his power, marched into Austria, annexed it to the Reich, and set in motion the steps that led to World War II.

Lawrence Levenson

Lawrence Levenson

240 Bradley Street
New Haven, CT 06510

Lawrence Levenson   Training and Supervising Analyst, Western New England Institute for Psychoanalysis; Assistant Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Yale University School of Medicine.

Theodore Jacobs needs no introduction to readers of the psychoanalytic literature. With his gift for bringing to life the drama of the consulting room in clear, engaging prose, he is for many of us a favorite psychoanalytic writer. Freud once observed that his case histories read like short stories, and the same can fairly be said of Ted Jacobs's writings.

Jacobs has now made the jump to the major leagues of literature with the publication of his first novel, The Year of Durocher, a coming-of-age story about three Jewish adolescents in 1948 New York City. Baseball aficionados will recall that Leo Durocher was the feisty, blunt manager who caused an uproar in the New York sports world during the summer of 1948 when he left as manager of the Brooklyn Dodgers to take over the reins as manager of the Dodgers' hated crosstown rivals, the New York Giants. Here Durocher's switch from the Dodgers to the Giants serves as a background metaphor for the story of love, friendship, and betrayal that unfolds among the three principal characters over the course of the novel.

The book opens with its narrator, Jonathan Manheim, suiting up for the first time with his high school varsity football team as it prepares for the big game against its main rival, Ryder. Jonathan's school, Harmon, is in Manhattan, Ryder in Brooklyn, so the game carries the added freight of the larger rivalry between the two boroughs. In the stands watching the game is a statuesque brunette with a Katharine Hepburn profile who catches Jonathan's attention during warm-ups. Jonathan plays well for most of the game, but on the final play he ingloriously fumbles the ball after a ferocious hit by Ryder's top player, Stan Schneiderman, sealing his team's defeat. For Jonathan, the fumble encapsulates the fumblings and defeats that he feels have come to define his life as a teenager, especially with girls.

The variety and depth of the contributions to this edited collection is perhaps the headline of this review. As with any edited volume, despite the excellent intentions and committed involvement of the editors, the contributions will vary in quality. As might be expected, the sections on outcome and process research include some of the strongest chapters. In particular, the Rabung and Leichsenring presentation of meta-analytic evidence is transparent regarding that methodology. However, since meta-analyses are often cited as compelling evidence of treatment effectiveness, there is a critically important omission from the discussion: an acknowledgment that meta-analysis does not provide conclusive evidence of effectiveness but rather signals that there is a compelling reason to do new, larger studies: “Even in aggregate, such results (meta-analysis) should be considered more as hypothesis formulating than as hypothesis testing. The main need … is for trials that are large enough to have adequate statistical power” (Hennekens and DeMets 2009). The excellence of the meta-analysis does not compensate for an overreliance on this method.

Sonya Martin

Sonya Martin

Sonya Martin  

Freud coined the term countertransference in letters to Jung in 1909 and first used it publicly in 1910, in “The Future Prospects of Psychoanalytic Therapy.” In both instances he was warning against the destructive potential of analysts' unconscious reactions to their patients. Writing a decade later in 1919, Ferenczi radically proposed that psychoanalysts could use their emotional responses to foster the empathy and insight underlying the “corrective emotional experience” that motivates therapeutic change. The ensuing century witnessed much debate over the desirable role, if any, of the feelings experienced by psychotherapists during patient encounters. Today most psychoanalysts acknowledge the utility of their own reactions for understanding their patients, citing both clinical experience and case studies to support their convictions. Now they have another resource at their disposal: in a recent article published in the American Journal of Psychiatry, Colli and colleagues have reported a systematic and statistically significant relationship between patients' personality traits and their therapists' reactions to them.

Colli et al. measured the correlation between therapists' emotional responses and patients' personality pathology in a random sample of 203 therapist-patient dyads in Italy engaged in weekly treatment of eight weeks' to six months' duration. The assessments consisted of the Therapist Response Questionnaire, which detects countertransference patterns, and the Shedler-Westen Assessment Procedure-200 (SWAP-200), which gauges personality and personality pathology. The primary outcome measure was the relationship between specific personality disorders (meeting DSM-IV-TR Axis II criteria) and the following countertransference reactions, attained via factor analysis: criticized/mistreated, helpless/inadequate, positive, parental/protective, overwhelmed/disorganized, special/overinvolved, sexualized, and disengaged. The secondary outcome measure was the relationship between countertransference reactions and therapist's theoretical orientation (i.e., psychodynamic versus cognitive-behavioral).

Glen Mazis

Glen Mazis

264 West Front Street
Marietta, PA 17547

Glen Mazis   Professor of Humanities and Philosophy, Penn State Harrisburg.

Lament of the Dead: Psychology after Jung's Red Book, published on August 26, 2013, is aptly titled in several ways. The book comprises a series of conversations between James Hillman (1926-2011), Director of Studies at the C. G. Jung Institute in Zurich from 1959 to 1969 and founder of the post-Jungian analytical school of archetypal psychology, and Sonu Shamdasani, Professor of Medical History at University College London and cofounder of the Philemon Foundation. Shamdasani labored for many years to edit the notebooks that make up the Red Book, having succeeded, in a three-year negotiation with Jung's heirs (who had kept the writings in a vault after his death), in obtaining their approval to publish them. He convinced the family that portions of the texts were accessible in the public domain and that it would be better were the entire work released in its best possible form.

The Red Book was written by Jung during a sixteen-year period after undergoing a psychological crisis at the age of thirty-eight. Jung worked on the notebooks, as well as the series of paintings and calligraphic renderings of figures in the text that are part of the work, from 1914 until 1930 (he would return to it later in life without completing the project). Hillman and Shamdasani's title suggests not only that an important part of the Red Book's narrative journey takes place among the dead, but also that the dead, still with us in their unfulfilled need to be heard, are largely ignored and that their voice is therefore plaintive. The title expresses even more so Hillman's and Shamdasani's conviction that the importance of the Red Book is to revise the way Jung's legacy to psychology is understood; if heeded, they believe, the book might alter the course of psychology. Finally, the title refers to the need to transform the understanding of Jung's idea of the archetype in light of the nature of the voice of the dead as they speak and act through us in unconscious ways (see Hoffman 2010).

With the long-awaited publication of Jung's Red Book: Liber Novus (see Hoffman 2010), James Hillman was given fertile ground in the last two years of his life to emphasize the reworking of Jungian ideas that were central to his project of archetypal psychology.

Malkah Notman   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Harvard Medical School; faculty, Boston Psychoanalytic Institute.

The mind/body duality has been with us in many forms: mind/brain, organic/functional, feelings/behavior. In psychoanalytic thinking the

918

actual body is often not quite present, with attention directed instead to fantasies and concepts about the body and its symbolic meanings. Relational perspectives consider the “embodied self,” for instance, but not the real three-dimensional body. In Unmasking Psychological Symptoms, Barbara Schildkrout approaches the mind/body duality with a particular focus. She asks us to consider the body as possibly an unrecognized contributor to our patients' emotional symptoms and complaints. Her emphasis is on finding any underlying somatic pathology that may play a role in problems that present as psychological. Fully aware of the complexity of syndromes, she avoids the dichotomous thinking that would reductively identify symptoms as either functional or organically based, to the exclusion of the other. Her thesis is that some somatic disorders can mimic psychological conditions, or produce psychological symptoms. For example, depression can accompany pancreatic cancer or thyroid malfunction. The depression is not the patient's reaction to the illness but rather is based on neuronal or hormonal changes attendant on the condition. If the therapist treats only the depression, the somatic problem can be missed. Schildkrout wants therapists to keep in mind the possibility that organic pathology may be present—say, a brain tumor, a seizure disorder, a thyroid malfunction—and to look for clues that arouse suspicion about the presence of covert organic illness. One needs to “unmask” psychological symptoms caused by a medical disorder in order to find that disorder. This point seems to me extremely important, and we as analysts and therapists can easily miss it, particularly when a patient's symptoms fit a specific psychiatric picture. The tendency then is to develop a dynamic formulation and look no further. This is particularly likely when the onset of a physical condition coincides with a major life event.

I wondered what kind of situations can make a clinician suspect organic illness. Schildkrout describes some important clues: inconsistencies in the description of symptoms, unusual timing, specific historical circumstances (e.g., a trip, an injury), an unconscious feeling in the therapist—a hunch that can be followed up.

From seemingly juxtaposed disciplines, Clara Mucci and Thomas Trezise assess an established canon in trauma theory and Holocaust studies that has influenced our thinking since the 1990s. Mucci, professor of literature and psychology at the University of Chieti (Italy), reviews psychoanalytic, neurological, and literary approaches to questions of trauma, mourning, and subjectivity. Trezise, an historian and scholar of French and Italian literature at Princeton, argues that theories of trauma and the Holocaust can obfuscate survivors' stories and limit both our ability to understand their experience and our knowledge of the subject.

World, Affectivity, Trauma: Heidegger and Post-Cartesian Psychoanalysis. By Robert D. Stolorow. New York: Routledge, 2011, xiv + 121 pp., $23.95.

M. Fromm

M. Fromm

P. O. Box 962
Stockbridge, MA 01262
E-mail: gerard.fromm@austenriggs.net

M. Fromm   Senior Consultant, the Erikson Institute, Austen Riggs Center; faculty, Massachusetts Institute for Psychoanalysis; faculty, the Yale Child Study Center; President, International Society for the Psychoanalytic Study of Organizations.

There is much to learn, much to like, much to ponder, and a few things to question in this slender, full-to-the-brim volume. To some degree, it feels as though its author, Robert Stolorow, has provided us a majestic coda to a long symphony—his exploration of intersubjectivity, trauma, and related subjects over many years—in which a few grand themes from previous movements return with crystal clarity and emotional conviction. The title itself has a kind of Mahlerian sweep—World, Affectivity, Trauma—that turns out to come directly from Heidegger, the Other with whom Stolorow is in deep conversation.

In his first sentence, Stolorow announces his aim here: “to show how Heidegger's existential philosophy enriches post-Cartesian psychoanalysis and how post-Cartesian psychoanalysis enriches Heidegger's existential philosophy” (p. 1).

[[RunningHead]]

R. Yehuda, N.P. Daskalakis, A. Lehrner, F. Desarnaud, H.N. Bader, I. Makotkine, J.D. Flory, L.M. Bierer, & M.J. Meaney (2014). Influences of maternal and paternal PTSD on epigenetic regulation of the glucocorticoid receptor gene in Holocaust survivor offspring.American Journal of Psychiatry171:872-880.

Sonya Martin

Sonya Martin

Sonya Martin  

The transmission of human experience from one generation to the next has occupied the minds of thinkers from Aristotle and Plato to Darwin and Freud and beyond. In recent years, genetics and epigenetics have figured into the explanation with increased prominence and nuance. Coined in 1942 by the British philosopher and biologist C. H. Waddington,

1101

the term epigenetics refers to heritable changes in phenotype that—unlike those of genetics—do not involve alterations to the underlying DNA sequence. Epigenetics allows for events in the mother's or the father's life to be passed along to their offspring at a molecular genetic level. Recently, epigenetic research has moved into the realm of psychiatry to help account for the intergenerational transmission of traumatic experience. This new research has deep implications for psychoanalysis.

In a recent study published in the American Journal of Psychiatry, Yehuda and colleagues measured the distinct influences of maternal and paternal PTSD on psychiatric illness (phenotype), DNA methylation (epigenetics), and glucocorticoid sensitivity (proposed as a link between phenotype and epigenetics) in 80 adult offspring of Holocaust survivors and 15 demographically matched controls. Recognizing that increased glucocorticoid sensitivity confers greater susceptibility to anxiety and depression, the researchers hypothesized that inheritable patterns in DNA methylation would impact the glucocorticoid receptor in ways that would increase the rate of PTSD among adult offspring.

The investigators assessed psychiatric illness via the Structured Clinical Interview for DSM-IV (SCID) and psychometric tests administered by clinical psychologists for anxiety, depression, dissociation, relationship style, childhood trauma, and perceived emotional health. They calculated DNA methylation of the glucocorticoid receptor promoter gene by PCR amplification of 39 base pair sites in mononuclear cells obtained from morning blood samples. Finally, they performed dexamethasone suppression testing to quantify baseline cortisol level and determine the sensitivity of glucocorticoid receptors.

Randall Paulsen   Assistant Professor of Psychiatry, Harvard Medical School; Consultant, Osher Clinical Center, Brigham and Women's Hospital.

Anthony Molino, a practicing psychoanalyst, anthropologist, and literary translator, has given us a marvelous collection of essays. Like a skillful curator, he has carefully chosen his title to convey the spirit of this book: Crossroads in Psychoanalysis, Buddhism and Mindfulness: The Word and the Breath. I had the image of Molino meeting me at the front door of a large villa in Tuscany, and inviting me in to explore at my own pace. In each room I would find an author who would tell me of his or her experience. He has chosen authors who are speaking from their practices, from their lives, which include both active meditation and some form of psychoanalytic therapy or healing work. You sense the authenticity of the chapters as you read them.

JAPA New Author Prize

JAPA's New Author Prize recognizes significant contributions to the journal by new authors. The prize is awarded for the best published manuscript by a new author, and includes a $500 honorarium that is presented at the Winter Meetings of the American Psychoanalytic Association.

A new author is considered to be someone who has not previously published in JAPA or any other major psychoanalytic journal and whose psychoanalytic career is in its early stages. The editor-in-chief has established a mentoring/editorial group to review submissions by new authors and assist in developing manuscripts for publication in JAPA.

Manuscripts must be submitted via the usual JAPA submission process using the online system accessed at http://mc.manuscriptcentral.com/japa. At the time of submission, an author may indicate in the cover letter that the manuscript is to be considered for the New Author Prize, and may ask that the paper be directed to the mentoring/editorial group for assistance in manuscript development. Final decision about publication remains with the editorial board and the editor-in-chief, as does the decision about selecting the prize-winning paper.

[[RunningHead]]

The Importance of Developing Rapport When Attempting to Treat an Oppositional-Defiant, Teenage Soiler in a Residential Setting

Michael Di Francesco and David Rodriguez

David Rodriguez

St. Christopher's Inc., Valhalla, New York
760 Jefferson Avenue, #7
Miami Beach, FL 33139
E-mail: mddifrancesco@gmail.com

David Rodriguez  

Encopresis, the repeated passage of feces into inappropriate places, is an underreported elimination disorder diagnosed primarily in children who have already been toilet trained (Murphy and Carney 2004). Understanding that this is an uncommon diagnosis for physically healthy individuals of average intelligence who have progressed beyond childhood, we hope to show the importance of conceptualizing and approaching a difficult case of this kind from a psychodynamic stance in order to develop rapport and a solid working relationship. Joey, our patient, is a highly symptomatic oppositional-defiant adolescent in residential care. While he suffers from long-term encopresis that has not significantly yielded to psychotherapeutic, psychopharmacological, or behavioral regimens, some progress has been made in the case due to a consistent psychodynamic conceptualization of the case and careful interventions. Notable is the clinically impressive disconnection between Joey's odd calmness and his heightened state of denial regarding the encopresis.

Joey was admitted to a residential facility at fourteen. His uncontrollable aggressive behavior at home and in school, as well as the encopresis, made it impossible to maintain him in a normal classroom. While Joey has always been of normal intelligence, now that he is eighteen and through his level of responsiveness to treatment, he has shown academic progression and has been able to express appropriate distress and insight regarding his chronic lack of bowel control. Secondary to his encopresis, Joey is highly anxious, struggles with chronic bouts of depression, is oppositional-defiant, and is apprehensive with regard to his future.

Joey's history and family dynamics are chaotic and complicated at best.

NP1

[[RunningHead]]

Defense Mechanisms and Cognitive Complaints: Assessing Cognitive Complaints and Self-Reported Defensive Styles

Megan Putnam , Lana Tiersky , Benjamin Freer and Michelle Pievsky

Michelle Pievsky

T-WH1-01
Williams Hall
1000 River Road
Teaneck, NJ 07666
E-mail: megan_putnam@student.fdu.edu

Michelle Pievsky  

Defense mechanisms, as theorized by ego psychologists, are the way in which the ego manages unpleasant affective urges, such as anxiety from intrapsychic conflict and stressors. These mechanisms are used to handle situations of inner turmoil for an individual and are unconscious reactions. Over time the individual, as managed by the ego, develops a pattern of behavior and reactions to this psychic distress that can be thought of as a defensive style.

NP11

Cognitive symptoms such as problems with attention or memory, or the inability to plan and organize, are among the complaints presented to clinicians by adult patients seeking treatment (de Braek, Dijkstra, and Jolles 2011). Various studies have indicated that the relationship between self-reported cognitive complaints does not correlate with objective deficits on the neuropsychological measures intended to identify that specific deficit (Wilhelm, Witthoft, and Schipolowski 2010). This discrepancy may in part be explained by the particular characteristics of the person reporting the complaint. Personal factors may contribute to the types of symptoms a patient reports. For example, the personality style trait of neuroticism has been found in one study (Wilhelm, Witthoft, and Schipolowski 2010) to be associated with an increase in scores on the Cognitive Failures Questionnaire (CFQ; Broadbent et al. 1982). Where the Defense Style Questionnaire (DSQ-40; Andrews, Singh, and Bond 1993) uses the term neurotic to represent a particular approach to defensive style, it should not be confused with the personality trait neuroticism. Neuroticism as a personality style may be defined as a “tendency toward enduring or frequent states of anxiety and nervousness, [and] may negatively color self-judgments” (Mascherek et al. 2011, p. 187). Studies have also demonstrated that symptoms of depression are related to self-report of cognitive problems (Wong, Wettemeck, and Klein 2000; Otto et al. 1994). Further, there has been some evidence of an increase in avoidance behaviors (Kennedy, Schwab, and Hyde 2001) and disengagement-oriented coping (Nicolas et al. 2013) with increased symptoms of depression. These behaviors may similarly reflect strategies used by those with an immature defensive style.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Defensive Style and Alexithymia in a Sample of Cognitive Complainers

Michelle Pievsky , Leila Abuelhiga , Benjamin Freer , Megan Putnam and Lana Tiersky

Lana Tiersky

T-WH-01
Williams Hall
1000 River Road
Teaneck, NJ 07666
E-mail: mpievsky@student.fdu.edu

Lana Tiersky  

Alexithymia is a personality construct characterized by three factors: difficulty identifying feelings, difficulty describing feelings, and externally oriented thinking (Bagby, Taylor, and Parker 1994; Taylor and Bagby 2013). Several studies have found a strong positive relationship between alexithymia and immature defensive style and a moderate negative relationship between alexithymia and mature defensive style (Helmes et al. 2008; Kooiman et al. 1998; Parker, Taylor, and Bagby 1998; Kooiman, Raats, and Spinhoven 2008; Fukunishi, Numata, and Hattori 1994; Wise, Mann, and Epstein 1991; Bogutyn et al. 1999). To date, however, little research has examined the relationship between defensive style and each of the three factors in alexithymia. No studies have examined alexithymia in patients with cognitive complaints, a population that often present with an alexithymic profile. The present study examined the relationship between defensive style and alexithymia in a population of patients with such complaints.

We hypothesized that the relationship between TAS-20 total score and defensive styles that was found in students (Helmes et al. 2008; Parker, Taylor, and Bagby 1998; Bogutyn et al. 1999), outpatient psychiatric patients (Kooiman et al. 1998; Wise, Mann, and Epstein 1991), and patients who had myocardial infarction (Fukunishi, Numata, and Hattori 1994) would be replicated in this study.

NP16

JAPA New Author Prize

JAPA's New Author Prize recognizes significant contributions to the journal by new authors. The prize is awarded for the best published manuscript by a new author, and includes a $500 honorarium that is presented at the Winter Meetings of the American Psychoanalytic Association.

A new author is considered to be someone who has not previously published in JAPA or any other major psychoanalytic journal and whose psychoanalytic career is in its early stages. The editor-in-chief has established a mentoring/editorial group to review submissions by new authors and assist in developing manuscripts for publication in JAPA.

Manuscripts must be submitted via the usual JAPA submission process using the online system accessed at http://mc.manuscriptcentral.com/japa. At the time of submission, an author may indicate in the cover letter that the manuscript is to be considered for the New Author Prize, and may ask that the paper be directed to the mentoring/editorial group for assistance in manuscript development. Final decision about publication remains with the editorial board and the editor-in-chief, as does the decision about selecting the prize-winning paper.

[[RunningHead]]

Project Eva: Evaluation of Two Prevention Programs with High-Risk Children in Kindergarten

Marianne Leuzinger-Bohleber , Tamara Fischmann , Verena Neubert , Lorena Hartmann , Katrin Läzer , Nicole Pfenning-Meerkötter , Maria Schreiber and Peter Ackermann

Peter Ackermann

Beethovenplatz 1-3
60325 Frankfurt am Main
GERMANY
E-mail: m.leuzingrr-bohleber@sigmund-freud-institut.de

Peter Ackermann  

A large number of empirical studies illustrate the importance and efficacy of early prevention for children of kindergarten age. The literature reveals that securely attached children (compared to insecure ones) show better social behavior, less aggression, more creative problem solving skills, and better cognitive functioning in social interactions. However, these studies reveal little about the complex interplay of protective and high-risk factors in the early development of children. To better investigate this area, Project EVA addresses children exposed to various risk factors whose families come from problematic rural areas with high unemployment and migration rates and an overall low socioeconomic status.

Aims

Project EVA is designed to evaluate the effectiveness of two prevention programs with respect to the children's attachment patterns and psychosocial behavior. The two main research questions are (1) Does the psychoanalytically oriented program Frühe Schritte (Early Steps), which addresses the specific needs of the children and their families, lead to a significant decrease in psychopathological symptoms in children compared to the standardized prevention program called Faustlos (Fistless)? (2) Does Frühe Schritte increase the probability of a change in attachment pattern from insecure to secure compared to Faustlos?

NP31

Imagination from Fantasy to Delusion. By Lois Oppenheim. New York: Routledge, 2013, xxvi + 207 pp., $140.00 hardcover, $40.95 paperback.

Léon Wurmser

Léon Wurmser

904 Crestwick Road
Towson, MD 21286
E-mail: leonwurmser@verizon.net

Léon Wurmser   Training and Supervising Analyst, Contemporary Freudian Society; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, University of West Virginia.

In his final great novel, The Adolescent, Dostoyevsky (1875) writes: “A photograph very rarely resembles the person. That stands to reason since the person only rarely resembles himself. It is only for brief moments that the human face expresses a person's most characteristic thought, a person's essence. An artist studies a face and discovers in it that essence” (p. 461).

371

Thomas Mann refers to the Wesensbild, “the image of the essence,” recurrently in Joseph and His Brothers. In other passages of The Adolescent, Dostoyevsky uses the term blagoobraziye, which translates loosely as “blessing's image,” again an essence of goodness as image. Imagination, however, is rooted in imagery, and so is the Greek concept of phantasía, as in Aristotle's definition of shame as perí adoxías phantasía, a “fantasy [a perception, an image, an idea] of humiliation.”

The thought is: art presents a deeper reality that no photograph can represent. This insight leads directly to one of the central concerns of the work to be reviewed here: the dialectic between reality and imagination, their identity and difference: “In life as in art it is a matter of seeking that integrative point of arrival through the dialectical interplay between the real and the imaginary, the known and the fantasy, the symbolized and the enacted” (p. 18).

In the introduction to this interesting, often bewilderingly rich and complex, but at least for me quite difficult book, Lois Oppenheim, professor of French and chair of the Department of Modern Languages and Literature at Montclair State University and a psychoanalyst, starts out from the statement that “creative endeavor augments the very awareness of self on which the regulation of our internal environment depends.” She aims in this work to illustrate “the enhancement of self that creativity affords” by investigating “the relationship of imagination to the self as agent” (p. xi; here and throughout, emphasis in the original).

Right away she wades into profound philosophical waters: “The fundamental premise of this study is twofold: It is, first, that the imaginary is real. Where it differs from what we commonly take to be reality is in structure and in form. The imaginary in art, for example, is not illusionary for it is phenomenologically describable and even depictable…. No less real than the imaginary of art, and thus fantasy, is the imaginary of delusion, ascertainable in the very function it serves” (p. xi). Fantasy and delusion both deal with agency. “The second premise is that change, the enhancement of self through an increase in agency, is facilitated by the biology of reward: The pleasure of increased self-cohesion—of efficacy acquired through knowledge of, and the attribution of meaning to, the world—is ultimately the functional sine qua non of imaginative thought” (p. xii).

Language, Representation, Meaning, Resonances

Psychoanalytic Terms and Concepts. Edited by Elizabeth L. Auchincloss and Eslee Samberg. New Haven: Yale University Press, 2012, xxvi + 341 pp., $85.00.

Leon Hoffman

Leon Hoffman

167 East 67th Street
New York, NY 10065

Leon Hoffman   Director, Pacella Parent Child Center, New York Psychoanalytic Society; Training and Supervising Analyst, New York Psychoanalytic Institute.

This book, a major project undertaken by the American Psychoanalytic Association, is a revised edition of Psychoanalytic Terms and Concepts, edited by Burness E. Moore and Bernard D. Fine (1990; third edition). The first edition, published in 1967 and revised in 1968, was also edited by Moore and Fine. A version of the current editors' introduction to the volume was published in JAPA as “Psychoanalytic Lexicography: Notes from Two ‘Harmless Drudges’” (Samberg and Auchincloss 2010).

This volume consists of a thirteen-page introduction plus an alphabetical listing (encyclopedia style) of contemporary terms from a variety of theoretical perspectives in use in psychoanalytic discourse. The list of editorial board members, editorial assistants, committee chairs, and contributors reflects the range of theoretical perspectives today current in psychoanalysis. The sheer extensiveness of the list reflects the Herculean nature of the task the editors faced as they put together this volume. “This book,” they state, “represents the concerted efforts of a small army of people who devoted endless hours to its construction” (p. vii).

I would add that this small army certainly would not have succeeded without the efforts of its two generals, to whom we must tip our hats in gratitude. I had not previously read the version of their introduction that was published in JAPA, so reading it here for the first time was an intellectual delight; this essay should be required reading for every psychoanalytic student, regardless of experience or orientation.

The introduction is divided into several sections covering the history of lexicography, the history of psychoanalytic lexicography, the problem of psychoanalytic language, the problem of theoretical pluralism, and methodology. The editors provide us a fascinating history of lexicography (i.e., dictionary-making) dating back to the mid-third millennium B.C.

JAPA Prize Announcment

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association

JAPA Prize

The $1000 JAPA Prize is awarded to the author of the best paper published in the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association during the previous year. All publications, including review articles, research articles, essays and book essays, may be considered for the award. Candidates for the award are nominated by the members of the JAPA Editorial Board, with the final decision being made by the Editor-in-Chief.

JAPA New Author Prize

JAPA's $500 New Author Prize recognizes significant contributions to the journal by new authors. It is awarded for the best published manuscript by a new author, and is presented at the Winter Meetings of the American Psychoanalytic Association.

A new author is considered to be someone who has not previously published in JAPA or any other major psychoanalytic journal and whose psychoanalytic career is in its early stages. A mentoring/editorial group has been established to review submissions by new authors and assist in developing manuscripts for publication in JAPA.

Manuscripts must be submitted via the JAPA submission process, using the online system at http://mc.manuscriptcentral.com/japa. At the time of submission, authors should indicate in the cover letter that the paper is to be considered for the New Author Prize, and may ask that it be directed to the mentoring/editorial group for assistance in manuscript development. Final decision about publication remains with the Editorial Board and the Editor-in-Chief, as does selection of the annual prize-winning paper.

E. Driessen, H.L. VAN, F.J. Don, J. Peen, S. Kool, D. Westra, M. Hendriksen, R.A. Schoevers, P. Cuijpers, J.W. Twisk, & J.J. Dekker (2013). The efficacy of cognitive-behavioral therapy and psychodynamic therapy in the outpatient treatment of major depression: A randomized clinical trial.American Journal of Psychiatry170:1041-1050.

Sonya Martin

Sonya Martin

Sonya Martin  

Psychoanalysts have long celebrated the ameliorative effects of talk therapy on individual patients. Early practitioners of psychoanalysis relied primarily on case studies to demonstrate their treatment's efficacy: that Bertha Pappenheim regained her motor and sensory functions to become a prominent social worker and activist, for example, or that Herbert Graf conquered his fear of horses to produce world-class operas. Subsequent skeptics have challenged the purported benefits of psychoanalysis by suggesting that other interventions—or possibly no intervention at all—might have yielded similar outcomes. Meanwhile, advocates of newer treatment modalities such as cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) have relied increasingly on quantitative measures to illustrate their success. Now, in an article published in the American Journal of Psychiatry, Driessen and colleagues have reported a randomized controlled trial demonstrating that time-limited psychodynamic therapy is at least as effective as CBT.

Driessen et al. measured the benefit of sixteen sessions of individual manualized CBT or short-term psychodynamic supportive therapy for 341 Dutch adults, randomly assigned to a treatment condition, who met DSM-IV criteria for a major depressive episode and had Hamilton Depression Rating Scale (HAM-D) scores ≥ 14. The primary outcome measure was post-treatment remission rate (HAM-D score ≤ 7). Secondary outcome measures included HAM-D and depressive self-report (IDS-SR) scores at the end of treatment and one year post-treatment. HAM-D assessors were not blind to treatment condition but displayed no significant biases on sensitivity analysis of their treatment expectations. Severely depressed individuals (HAM-D score > 24) were also given pharmacological treatment with extended-release venlafaxine at daily doses of 75 to 225 mg, followed by citalopram or nortryptilline for adverse effects or non-response, at rates that did not differ significantly between treatment groups.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

I Sabotatori Interni: Il Funzionamento Delle Organizzazioni Patologiche di Personalità (Internal Saboteurs: The Functioning of Pathological Personality Organizations). By Francesco Gazzillo. Milan: Raffaello Cortina, 2012, xiv + 209 pp., €23.00 paperback.

Paolo Migone

Paolo Migone

Via Palestro, 14
43123 Parma
ITALY
E-mail: migone@unipr.it

Paolo Migone   Editor-in-Chief, Psicoterapia e Scienze Umane; Research Associate, American Psychoanalytic Association; member, Editorial Board, Psychological Issues book series; member, Executive Board, Italian chapter of the International Society for the Study of Personality Disorders; Fellow, American Academy of Psychoanalysis and Dynamic Psychiatry.

Francesco Gazzillo is a young researcher at the Sapienza University of Rome, with a strong interest in psychotherapy research, personality disorders, and diagnostic methods. He has worked on the Italian version of the Shedler-Westen Assessment Procedure (SWAP) and written empirical research papers based on this method for describing and diagnosing personality. He is also the author of a biography of Masud Khan and of other books on the history of psychoanalysis and psychoanalytic theories (Gazzillo and Fontana 2001; Gazzillo and Silvestri 2008; Gazzillo and Ortu 2013). Such diversified interests in a person are not common. In particular, the fact that Gazzillo is a psychotherapy researcher gives this book added value, because the various psychoanalytic theories of pathological personality organizations are seen through the eyes of a person conversant with scientific methodology.

As we know, the term internal saboteur was coined by Ronald Fairbairn (1952), who also used the term antilibidinal ego. Fairbairn postulated an early unitary ego that rather than seeking pleasure seeks a relationship with the object; environmental failures lead to compensatory internalization of the object, which is then defensively split. Thus, the phrase “internal saboteurs” in Gazzillo's title is powerfully evocative. It would be a mistake, however, to think that this book deals mostly with Fairbairn's theory of personality. Three quarters of it (the first three of its four chapters) consists of detailed reviews of the many contributions to the literature on pathological personality organizations. In the fourth and final chapter, summarizing these various conceptions, Gazzillo presents his own unitary view of the topic. However, even without this last chapter, the first three make the book well worth reading, providing as they do a bird's-eye view of the various theories in the difficult field of severe personality psychopathology.

[[RunningHead]]

The Therapist in Mourning: From the Faraway Nearby. By Anne J. Adelman and Kerry L. Malawista. New York: Columbia University Press, 2013, xx + 308 pp., $29.50 paperback.

Julia Matthews-Bellinger

Julia Matthews-Bellinger

19 Fair Oaks Park
Needham, MA 02492
E-mail: julia.matthewsmd@gmail.com

Julia Matthews-Bellinger   Associate Professor of Clinical Psychiatry, University of Massachusetts Medical School; Training and Supervising Analyst, Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute.

Borrowing a term from painting, Anne Adelman and Kerry Malawista, the editors of this volume, offer a visual image of mourning: “In the

1144

middle-distance, the mourner is caught between two worlds: in one, the lost person is still accessible, a specter just out of reach whose presence is palpable and real, and in the other, the world is irrevocably changed and rendered nearly unrecognizably void, the absence of the other the omnipresent backdrop for all experiences…. the middle-distance to be traversed is, in a sense, inhabited by the memory traces of those people, places, and parts of ourselves—of our ‘therapist-selves’—now lost to us” (pp. 10-11). “Traversing” this middle distance is always difficult and painful, but far more difficult alone, as is often the lot of therapists in mourning.

All human beings suffer losses and must mourn and adapt to change. Since Freud's remarkable 1917 paper, “Mourning and Melancholia,” much has been written about the psychological work of mourning, the factors that facilitate or interfere, and the nature and determinants of pathological mourning. Writers from diverse psychoanalytic traditions have expanded and deepened our understanding of the psychic work of mourning (Aragno 2003; Clewell 2004; Hewison 2008; Kernberg 2000, 2010; Loewald 1962; Ornstein 2010). The shift toward interpersonal and intersubjective perspectives in recent decades has further opened the discussion to a consideration of the essential involvement of others in the work of mourning, including the participation of a therapist or analyst in a patient's mourning during treatment. At the same time, as discussed by Robert Winer in this volume, mourning is increasingly understood as central to any analytic process (see also Akhtar 2001; Glennon 2004; Kogan 2007; Sollars 2004; Frommer 2005).

This book makes a valuable contribution to a contemporary perspective on the analyst's experience within the therapeutic situation. It is a fundamental truth that therapists, like their patients, suffer and mourn losses. Some of these losses, such as a death of a parent, spouse, friend, or child, are private; some are more public, such as the loss of a colleague or an institution; and some are intrinsic to the work as a therapist, such as the loss of a patient through termination or, more exceptionally, death. But all losses can affect the therapist's clinical work.

responseSet.title: Maternal Reliance: Commentary on Kristeva
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Maternal Reliance: Commentary on Kristeva

Mitchell Wilson

Mitchell Wilson

2960A Piedmont Avenue
Berkeley, CA 94705
E-mail: mdwmd@comcast.net

Mitchell Wilson  

In “Reliance, or Maternal Eroticism,” Julia Kristeva conveys, as throughout her work on the maternal, a persistent desire to speak from a forbidden place (an “outlaw” place), at least within the context of normative psychoanalytic theory. With hints of the alchemical, she mixes a deeply scholarly sensibility with wisdom, sobriety, and passion. In my remarks I will situate her work within the basic Lacanian categories—especially the symbolic and the real—because it is within this psychoanalytic context that Kristeva's project emerges. Kristeva, with only symbolic tools at her disposal, seeks the real, which she associates with the feminine, the maternal. By way of Freud and Lacan and the specimen dream of psychoanalysis, the dream of Irma's injection, I'll consider why the real and the feminine as forbidden object are so intimately linked. Next I'll discuss (all too briefly) Kristeva's picture of maternal passion, not only in relation to the real, but also in connection with contemporary ideas about maternal capacities such as holding and containing. Maternal passion involves an ethical position, what Kristeva calls herethics. This herethical position has direct implications for the analyst as a subject of responsibility. I conclude with a postscript that is meant to be more allusive than assertive, more provocative than definitive.

For French analysts especially, and many Freudians more generally, the structuration of the mind, family, and society-writ-large rests on the father's role, on what is typically called the paternal function. The mother, as French analysts like to emphasize, already instantiates thirdness via identification with the “father of individual prehistory” (Aisenstein 2012). Thirdness evolves as the mother-infant dyad is gradually triangulated by the father. This triangulation, instituted by the prohibition of incest (the father's “no”) is, of course, the oedipal situation. Thus, the mother as primary, primitive object is forbidden. It is important to appreciate that the oedipal situation is symbolically structured.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Effective Treatment Strategies for Autism during the First Five Years of Development

Elizabeth Levey

Elizabeth Levey

32 Fruit Street
Boston, MA 02114
E-mail: ejlevey@gmail.com

Elizabeth Levey  

There is a gap between, on the one hand, psychoanalytic theory regarding treatment for autism that recognizes both relational and body-based treatment and, on the other, conventional psychoanalytic treatment. Recent psychoanalytically based empirical research data indicate that treatment strategies exist to address the ineffective and pathological coping strategies present in infants and children with autism spectrum disorders (ASDs). This panel, chaired by Stephen Seligman, used videotape clips of therapy sessions over the course of a multi-year treatment of a child beginning at ten months, as well as verbal presentation of case material by the treating psychoanalyst, Molly Romer Witten. Gilbert Foley, a psychoanalyst, and Gerard Costa, a developmental psychologist and infant mental health specialist, were discussants; together they laid out the principles of the change process and illustrated the complex issues present early in life for children who present with behavior on the autism spectrum.

Seligman began by introducing the topic of the psychoanalytic theory of autism by reviewing past and current controversies. The problem with the “refrigerator mother” theory, which postulated that autism is an infant's defensive response to a cold, rejecting mother, was that it defined the problem unidimensionally. The pendulum has since swung in the other direction, and a new unimensional error is currently favored: the attribution of primary causal influence to neurobiological deficits in the infant. The truth, Seligman said, involves the interaction of endowment and environment.

Panel held at the Winter Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, January 18, 2014. Panelists: Stephen Seligman (chair), Molly Romer Witten, Gilbert Foley, Gerard Costa.

Arlene Richards

200 East 89th Street

Arlene Richards   Training and Supervising Analyst, Contemporary Freudian Society and the International Psychoanalytical Association; Fellow, Institute for Psychoanalytic Training and Research.

Dear reader: Before reading this review you should know that I am cited in this book, and quoted once, though briefly. I will try to be as objective as I can. You be the judge of whether I have succeeded.

This may be the most practically important book on psychoanalytic therapy published this year. It amounts to an assertion that we cannot in the name of caution ignore or defer the use of modern technology in our work. The various chapters cover the subject from points of view ranging from mild disapproval of the dangers of internet addiction as a psychological illness to recommending use of the internet for treatment at the highest, or deepest, level. All of the contributors advocate that we accept, with due caution, communication via telephone and the internet as a reality that has changed what patients today, especially younger ones, find comfortable and useful. Yet most of these authors also believe that the wisdom of accepting these parameters must be further researched, discussed, and evaluated.

Most of the contributors to this volume use the phrase “face to face” to describe treatment in the analyst's or therapist's office. Yet analysis is not “face to face” when the patient lies on the couch. And it is “face to face” when both participants in the conversation are looking at each other's faces on screens that are eighteen to twenty inches in front of them. It is more intimately face to face than most conversations—at times even strangely, disturbingly so. Yet Freud's innovation of having a conversation while one person looks at the wall or the ceiling, with the other looking at the top of that person's head, was surely more strange to his patients than use of the telephone or computer is to ours. Those who consider Freud the founder of a valuable form of psychological treatment may do him the greatest honor by following his example of experimenting to find what works.

I enjoyed and learned from the clinical vignettes—from analysts, psychotherapists, and especially patients.

responseSet.title: Edith Wharton's Threshold Phobia and Two Worlds
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Edith Wharton's Threshold Phobia and Two Worlds

Deanna Holtzman and Nancy Kulish

Nancy Kulish

625 Purdy Street
Birmingham, MI 48009
E-mail: nkulish@aol.com

Nancy Kulish  

Oedipus complex, phobia, sexuality, conflict, female psychology

The American novelist Edith Wharton suffered an unusual childhood neurotic symptom, a fear of crossing thresholds, a condition that might be called a “threshold phobia.” This symptom is identified and examined in autobiographical material, letters, diaries, and selected literary fiction and nonfiction left by Wharton to arrive at a formulation not previously drawn together. A fascinating theme—living or being trapped between “two worlds”—runs through much of the writer's life and work. The phobia is related to this theme, and both can be linked more broadly to certain sexual conflicts in women. This understanding of Wharton's phobia, it is argued, throws new light on the developmental issues and conflicts related to the female “oedipal” or triadic phase, characterized by the need to negotiate the two worlds of mother and of father.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Research on the Relation of Psychoanalysis and Neuroscience: Clinical Meaning and Empirical Science

Virginia Barry and Charles Fisher

Charles Fisher

111 North Wabash Avenue
Chicago IL 60602
E-mail: vcbarry@gmail.com

Charles Fisher  

This report, which inaugurates a new format for JAPA, is presented to honor the work of Howard Shevrin, who since 1962 has contributed to the psychiatric, neuroscientific, and psychoanalytic literature on the interface between clinical meaning and empirical science. Highlights of his work include his 1968 publication in Science, “Visual Evoked Response Correlates of Unconscious Mental Processes” (Shevrin and Fritzler 1968), his 1993 plenary address to the American Psychoanalytic Association, “Is Psychoanalysis One Science, Two Sciences, or No Science at All? A Discourse among Friendly Antagonists” (Shevrin 1995), and distinguished work on primary process, memory, conscious and unconscious processes, and the value and power of a comprehensive psychoanalytic theory. Shevrin's Dream Interpreters: A Psychoanalytic Novel in Verse (2003) moves us dramatically through the inner realms of his characters, giving his readers an introduction to the psychoanalytic experience.

Some psychoanalysts say that interdisciplinary work such as Shevrin's cannot address clinical meaning. Some cognitive scientists say that this kind of work cannot rise to the level of neuroscientific proof. The research reported here counters both statements.

Howard Shevrin and Michael Snodgrass, both of the University of Michigan (where Shevrin, a member of the Michigan Psychoanalytic Institute, is director of the Ormond and Hazel Hunt Laboratory for the Study of Conscious and Unconscious Processes)

Report on research presented at a discussion group at the Spring Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Chicago, Friday, June 6, 2014. Presenters: Howard Shevrin and Michael Snodgrass. Moderators: Charles Fisher and Richard Kessler.

성격장애 환자 치료에서의 핵심 문제점: (CORE ISSUES IN THE TREATMENT OF PERSONALITY-DISORDERED
            PATIENTS)
responseSet.authorMast: 댄 에이치 뷰이 and  Dan H. Buie
responseSet.authorCitation: 뷰이, 댄. 에. & Buie, D. H.
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

성격장애 환자 치료에서의 핵심 문제점

(CORE ISSUES IN THE TREATMENT OF PERSONALITY-DISORDERED PATIENTS)

댄 뷰이 and Dan Buie

Dan Buie

Dan Buie  

우리 모두는 성격 장애 환자들과의 임상 경험에서 같거나 비슷한 경험을 한다. 또한 우리는 그들의 특성이 확실하게 나타나 있다는 사실에 주목하고 있다. 이 환자들은 그들이 누구인가 하는 것을 아는 측면에서 뚜렷할 정도로 정신적으로 방황하고 있다: 그들은 음울한 외톨이임을 스스로 느끼고 있다; 가치가 없다는 암울한 느낌도 가지고 있다; 그들 스스로에 대해서 어떤 따뜻함도 느끼지 못하고 있다; 그리고 그들은 자신이 다른 사람과의 관계에서 진실한 입장을 가진 온전한 한 사람인 것을 느끼지 못하고 있다. 우리는 이러한 특성을 그들이 인간의 안정성, 심지어는 생존 능력에 기본이 되는 다섯 가지 능력에 결핍이 있어서 그런 것이라고 말할 수 있겠다. 그것은 자기를 유지하기 위해서 필요하므로, 나는 그것을 자기 유지 능력으로 부르려고 한다.

그것은 다음과 같은 것을 포함한다 (1) 자기 실재(감)에 대한 능력, 자신의 특성 그리고 자신과 다른 사람과의 관계를 현실로 즉 실제적인 존재로서 (인지적으로만 아는 것이 아니라) 경험적으로 아는 것; (2) 자기 안전(감)에 대한 능력, 자신이 안전하다라고 지속적으로 경험 할 수 있는 능력, 이것은 안전하고 따뜻하게 보듬어주는 환경에서 자라난 경우에 물려받은 유산이다; (3)자기 가치(감)에 대한 능력, 자신의 자기 가치를 경험적으로 알고 있는 것이다; (4) 자기 자신에 대한 사랑을 경험하는 능력; 그리고 (5) 자신의 정체성을 경험적으로 아는 능력이다. 이것을 요약한다면, 사람은 그들이 실존하며, 외톨이(가 되는 것으)로부터 안전하며, 귀중한 가치가 있고 또한 그들 스스로의 사랑을 받을 만하며, 한편 그들이 정체성을 가지고 있다는 것을 경험할 필요가 있다는 것이다.

신경증적인 환자와 성격 장애 환자 사이의 주된 차이로는 신경증적인 환자는 그들 자신에게 기본적인 자기 경험들을 제공할 능력을 지니고 있다는 점을 들 수 있다. 성격 장애 환자들은 그러하지 못하다. 그러므로 성격 장애 환자들과의 치료 목표는 그들이 이러한 능력을 발달시킬 수 있게 돕는 것이다.

NP1

responseSet.title: Raids on the Unsayable: Talk in Psychoanalysis
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Raids on the Unsayable: Talk in Psychoanalysis

Lewis Kirshner

Lewis Kirshner

3517 Duck Pond Road
Waterford, VT 05819
E-mail: lewis_kirshner@hms.harvard.edu

Lewis Kirshner  

It is indeed because something has been knotted to something like speech that the discourse can undo it.

—Jacques Lacan (1957-1958)

Psychoanalysis is the “talking cure,” by definition, but how we understand this depends on how we understand language and its relation to conscious thought, the unconscious, and subjectivity. These are complex and contested matters. Here I wish to make three points, First, I follow a long tradition that identifies self-reflexive conscious thought with language and speech. Second, I oppose any substantive notion of the unconscious as a repository of repressed representations—wishes or fantasies—and its corollary whereby therapeutic action is viewed in terms of the detection and interpretation of these unconscious meanings. Instead, I highlight a conception of the unconscious that refers to what cannot be symbolically represented and made available to thought. Finally, I support the position that subjectivity lives in language and is maintained by dialogue, in tandem with nonverbal expressions. For each of these points I will make connections to clinical practice.

The Role of Language in Conscious Thought and Speech

We can look back as far as Plato (in the Sophist) to link thinking to language. It is easy to forget when we are sitting with patients that we are engaged in exchanges of words, not just talking about important matters to which our words refer. In ordinary relationships we may assume that we share common references in our conversations, but in psychoanalysis we know that is not necessarily the case.

Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Harvard Medical School; Training and Supervising Analyst, Boston Psychoanalytic Institute.

Submitted for publication July 1, 2014.

responseSet.title: A Kid Like Jake. By Daniel Pearle. Lincoln Center Theater, New York, 2013.
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Kid Like Jake. By Daniel Pearle. Lincoln Center Theater, New York, 2013.

Mark O'Connell

Mark O'Connell

304 Park Avenue South, #1106
New York, NY 10010
E-mail: markoconnelltherapist@gmail.com

Mark O'Connell   Board Member, Institute for Human Identity; private practice, New York.

What if Cinderella attends the wrong ball? What if she's asked to catch a ball, instead of dance, and is ridiculed for refusing? What if Cinderella is a four-year-old boy named Jake whose parents desperately want him to get into an elite private school?

These are among the questions raised by A Kid Like Jake, a new play by Daniel Pearle that had its world premiere at Lincoln Center Theater in 2013. The play presented a topical but largely unsung dilemma: how to raise such a boy in the face of norms more wicked than a fairy-tale stepmother.

Much like the parents in the play, psychotherapists are awakening to gender nonconformity and the pressing needs it places on us as practitioners: to challenge tradition, welcome new voices, and expand our capacity to hold and reflect forms of expression, identity, and self-narratives that have no precedent. Daily, we are challenged by the ever increasing number of “gender-creative” children and adults as they arrive at our offices and transcend our culture's normative definitions of male/female (Ehrensaft 2011).

[[RunningHead]]

Growth and Turbulence in the Container/Contained: Bion's Continuing Legacy. Edited by Howard B. Levine and Lawrence J. Brown. Hove, East Sussex: Routledge, 2013, xvi + 323 pp., $160.00 hardcover, $49.95 paperback.

Brian Tobin

Brian Tobin

291 Whitney Avenue, #404
New Haven, CT 06511
E-mail: brian.tobin@yale.edu

Brian Tobin   Assistant Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Yale School of Medicine; faculty, Western New England Institute for Psychoanalysis.

As I was recently deepening my study of Bion's work, I was stunned to read his opening statement in the Tavistock seminars: “It took me a long time to realize that the actual experience of being psychoanalyzed was a traumatic one and it takes a long time to recover from it” (Bion 2005, p. 1). If psychoanalysis is traumatic for the analysand, is it also for the analyst? I imagine Bion would answer unequivocally yes.

For Bion the ontogenesis of thinking is infused with trauma. His classic metaphor of the mother-infant dyad highlights how the capacity to think unfolds and where the trauma lies. Bion believes that the infant is filled with overwhelming feelings and sensations suitable only for evacuation. Ogden (2004) reminds us of Bion's elegant yet simple ideas about the ontogenesis of thinking. He states that in the earliest stages of life (and in working with primitive sectors of an analysand's mind) it takes two people to think.

LaFarge (2001) explains this as follows: “Beset by affects and sensations that he [the infant] cannot tolerate or make sense of, the infant evokes these feelings in the mother” (p. 67). Implicit in this model of thinking is the trauma the mother withstands by being open and receptive to the tidal waves of raw emotion from her child. In analogous fashion, the analyst is at times bombarded with verbiage from the analysand, whose mind is not symbolically communicating lexically framed thoughts using language, but rather is expelling primitive feelings into the analyst. And the mother-analyst endures all this to help the child-analysand learn to think for him- or herself.

One of Bion's seminal constructs is that of the container/contained. This concept embraces the interpersonal developmental experience of learning to use one's own mind for thinking, especially in sectors of the mind where thinking has been stunted or has barely existed. What the mother-analyst endures on behalf of her child will one day become (if all goes well) an intrapsychic capacity of the child to tolerate its own raw emotions and to use them for thinking and learning. Our capacity to learn from raw emotional experience hinges on our capacity to absorb and make sense of potentially traumatic and overwhelming emotions.

[[RunningHead]]

Representational and Encoding Processes: Dissociated Mode of Mentalization

Lily Rothschild-Yakar , Ariela Waniel and Daniel Stein

Daniel Stein

Haifa 31905
ISRAEL
E-mail: lilyrot@yahoo.com

Safra Children's Hospital, Sheba Medical Center, Tel Hashomer, Israel, and Department of Education, Bar Ilan University

Safra Children's Hospital, Sheba Medical Center, Tel Hashomer, Israel, and Sackler Faculty of Medicine, Tel-Aviv University

Daniel Stein  

The model of mentalization provides a formulation for normal development of modes of experience and psychopathology. Mentalization refers to the capacity to reflect and interpret one's own behavior and the behavior of others, as caused by intentional internal mental states such as thoughts, feelings, and beliefs (Fonagy et al. 1998). The domain of mentalization is multifaceted, including four dimensions: implicit/explicit, cognitive/affective, internally/externally-based mentalization, and mentalizing self/others' experiences (Fonagy and Luyten 2009).

The ability to mentalize develops from three early modes of representing the internal world: In the earliest, the psychic equivalence mode, the infant does not differentiate between internal and external, his or her mind with the minds of others. In the pretend mode, the child is able to maintain an “as if” private reality totally dissociated from the shared external world. The teleological mode is a mode of thinking that equates desires and feelings with observable physical behavior.

A major factor in helping children arrive at the reflective mode is the caregiver's accurate and “marked”/differentiated mirroring of the infant's subjective experiences in a secure attachment relationship (Fonagy et al. 2002).

NP24

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

(사람에게) 거리를 두고 있는 환자들에게 있어서의 정체성 장애

Identity Disturbance In Distant Patients

어윈 호프만 and Irwin Hoffmann

Irwin Hoffmann

1240 Westlake Boulevard, Suite 211
Weststlake Village, CA 91361
E-mail: irwinhoffman@la.twcbc.com

Irwin Hoffmann  

만성적으로 (사람에게)거리를 두고 있고, 감정적으로 고립된 환자들은 종종 정체성 장애를 보인다. 학자들의 주장에 의하면 정체성은 나르시시즘이라는 주제적 패턴으로 발달하는데, 이 패턴은 아이의 자기감에 미치는 엄마의 조기 리비도적인 영향의 성격에 따라 형성된다. 정체성은, 나는 누구인가? 하는 질문에 답하는 자기-정의의 한 형태다. 이러한 환자들의 치료에서, 나르시시즘적인 취약성에 관한 저항들, 즉 나르시시즘적인 저항은 환상에 불과한 생각이지만 안정감을 제공하여 분석가로 하여금 이 핵심의 병리 문제에 주의를 기울이지 않고 회피할 수 있도록 한다: 그것은 다른 사람을 향한 원초적이면서 무서울 정도의 욕구들, 다른 사람에 관한 무의식의 공상들, 다른 사람에 대한 의존, 또한 다른 사람을 위해서 기능하는 것(기능성)을 말한다. 환자들이 자신들의 의존성을 다루는 소재(내용, 자료)와 치료적 상호작용을 회피하는 것은 내적인 안정성에 관한 그들의 중요한 문제를 다루는 것을 배제하기 위해서 분석가와 암묵의 계약을 맺으면서 일어나는 측면이 있기도 하다. 이러한 문제들 중에는 침입에 대한 불안과 내적 안정성에 관한 불안이 있다. 분석이 진행됨에 따라서, 그러한 환자의 정체성의 요소들은 명료화되게 되며 또한 분석가와 환자 양자에게 있어서 그 문제는 이해가 되고 정리가 된다. 이것은 환자로 하여금 개별성을 띤, 더 체현화되고 생생한 경험들을 이야기하게 할 수 있다. 이러한 접근 방법을 사용한 한 환자의 분석을 예시하기 위해서 하나의 사례를 제시하겠다.

[[RunningHead]]

정신분석에서의 정체를 어떻게풀어서진행이되게할까

How To Help Get Stuck Analyses Unstuck

레베카 채플란 and Rebecca Chaplan

Rebecca Chaplan

40 West 77th Street, 1F
New York, NY 10024
E-mail: rc361@columbia.edu

Rebecca Chaplan  

솔깃하지만 다소 오해를 불러 일으킬 수 있는 표제를 가진 이 패널은 세 가지 사례를 제시하였다. 세 가지 사례에서 분석가는, 진전이 없어서 즉, 실행화된(enacted) 공상을 환자가 분석적으로 전혀 이해하지 않으려 하는 모습에 좌절하여 거의 모든 희망을 잃고 있었다. 세 가지 사례에서 그처럼 “곤경에 빠져서 정체되어 멈추어 있다는 것”은 환자가 무의식적으로 분석가에게 할당한 역할에 분석가가 깊게 빠져있는 경험을 하고 있는 바와 같이, 분석가와 환자 사이에 서로 함께 공유하고 있는 역동에 의해 일어나는 것으로 이해되었다. 이러한 역전이 반응을 사용하고 무엇이 실행화되고 있는지를 생각하는 분석가의 능력이 변화에 저항하는 힘들을 이해하는 하나의 길을 제공하였다. 우리는 이 세 가지의 사례가 환기시키는 병력에서 분석가가 환자의 세계관 내에서부터, 곤경에 빠져서 정체되려는 패턴으로의 인력적인 끌림에 저항하기 위한 해결책을 발견하고 있으며, 그 해결책이 각 사례에 특정되어 있지만 다소간 공유하는 원칙에 기반을 두고 있음을 보게 된다. 그러므로 활발한 상호작용 및 이견이 거의 없었던 오전의 토의에서 도출된 생각들은 조언을 어떻게 제공할 것인가에 관해서라기보다는 이 세 가지 사례의 공통점이 무엇이며 또한 무엇이 도움이 되었는지에 관한 생각들이었다.

로즈메리 발삼(Rosemary Balsam)은 좌장으로서 패널 토론자들을 소개하였다. 발삼은 우리가 구어체로 지칭하는 바 “곤경에 빠져서 정체되어 멈추어 있음(우리말 구어체로는 ‘빼도박도 못함’)”은 하나의 임상적 실체이며, 이는 “교착 상태,” “부정적인 치료적 반응,” “실행화(enactment)

미국 정신분석학회 봄 학회에서 행해진 패널임. 시카고, 2012. 6. 17. 패널리스트: 로즈메리 발삼(좌장), 스탠리 코언, 프랜시스 아놀드, 디온느 파웰.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Ever Evolving Approaches to Exploring the Human Mind

Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, 5Th Edition. Washington, DC: American Psychiatric Association, 2013, 991 pp.Desk Reference to the Diagnostic Criteria from DSM-5. Washington, DC: American Psychiatric Association, 2013, 443 pp.

Leon Hoffman

Leon Hoffman

167 East 67th Street
New York, NY 10065
E-mail: hoffman.leon@gmail.com

Leon Hoffman   Director, Pacella Parent Child Center, and co-Director, Research Center, New York Psychoanalytic Society and Institute.
DSM, RDoC, biopsychosocial, narrative medicine, mental function and dysfunction, cognitive and neuroscience, dynamic interplay, Disruptive Mood Dysregulation Disorder (DMDD)

The production of the fifth edition of Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM-5™) was, according to the volume's introduction, a twelve-year process with many complex interactions among the editors and a variety of mental health practitioners and researchers, both proponents and critics of the process. I will describe the organization of DSM-5, address some critical evaluations of the new edition and how it was created, and compare it to the NIMH Research Domain Criteria (RDoC). I will address the volume's value and limitations (the latter include its lack of a narrative section and its jettisoning use of the Defensive Functioning Scale and the Global Assessment Functioning Scale, which should have been replaced with the Global Assessment of Relational Functioning Scale). I propose that the biopsychosocial model can be integrated into psychiatric nosology via the incorporation of principles from narrative medicine. As an example of in-depth analysis of all the disorders catalogued in DSM-5, I briefly discuss the new and controversial diagnosis of Disruptive Mood Dysregulation Disorder (DMDD) to illustrate the value and the limits of DSM-5.

responseSet.title: Introduction to Julia Kristeva
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Introduction to Julia Kristeva

Bonnie Litowitz

Bonnie Litowitz

Bonnie Litowitz  

In 2011 Julia Kristeva presented a paper, “Reliance, ou l'erotisme maternel,” at the seventy-first Congres des Psychanalystes de Langue Française. In October 2013 she presented an English translation of that paper at the Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis. That translation, which appears in this issue of JAPA, was prepared by Rachel Boué Widawsky, a former student (and later a colleague) of Kristeva at Université Denis Diderot (Paris VII), and Perry Zurn, a doctoral candidate in French philosophy at DePaul University.1

As Anglo-American psychoanalysis has increasingly turned its attention to the study of the earliest developmental period—the one called “preoedipal”—our emphasis has been predominantly on the child's experience of the mother. But what of the mother's subjective experience of these momentous events—conceiving, carrying, giving birth to (separating from) and caring for her child? Kristeva's essay is an attempt to rescue the subjectivity of the mother as a subject for psychoanalytic inquiry by directing our attention to the mother's bodily responses to these most common experiences in a woman's life.

Kristeva's exploration of the mother's body is rooted in her grounding in Freud's writing but also incorporates her extensive reading of post-Freudian theorists—Melanie Klein, Jacques Lacan, André Green, Jean Laplanche, and others—with whom she has critically engaged in her own writing. She also brings to her exploration of maternal eroticism insights from her work across the many disciplines in which she is a scholar: linguistics, philosophy, literature, and religion. As a consequence, some aspects of her approach to her topic, including her conceptual vocabulary, may be unfamiliar to some JAPA readers. For example, terms such as the semiotic and ab-ject(ion) were coined by Kristeva in the course of earlier studies and now are brought to bear on this topic. In light of this intertextuality in her writing, we have asked one of the translators, Rachel

1The French text was published as “L' erotisme maternel” in Pulsions de Temps (Paris: Fayard, 2013, pp. 197-214).

responseSet.title: The Interpretive Process: The Power of “Mere” Words
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Interpretive Process: The Power of “Mere” Words

Alan Spivak

Alan Spivak

11980 San Vicente Boulevard, Suite 712
Los Angeles, CA 90049
E-mail: apspivak@hotmail.com

Alan Spivak  

Anna O. coined the term “talking cure” while telling Breuer, her doctor, of the healing effect of her “chimney-sweepings.” The test of time has shown, however, that a cure that endures rarely is achieved so easily. Indeed, it was the limitations Freud found in the method of abreaction that inspired him to invent the more nuanced and complex treatment of psychoanalysis. In this revision of the talking cure, much of the healing is thought to be set in motion by the doctor's “translation” of the patient's speech.

I will begin by examining Freud's conception of the link between cure and translation, his word for interpretation. I will then explore curative aspects of the analyst's talking that were essentially unmentioned by Freud, but that may magnify exponentially the transformative power of the analyst's words. Finally, I will illustrate, with a vignette, the power of interpretation to deepen the analytic process and transform an analysand's relation to herself and to her mind.1

In The Interpretation of Dreams Freud (1900) quotes the French psychologist Maury: “It is our impulses that are speaking and making us act” (p. 73 n. 1). Later, in a letter to Jung, Freud (1906) developed this idea into what would become a guiding principle for psychoanalytic treatment: “Transference provides the impulse necessary for understanding and translating the language of the ucs.; where it is lacking, the patient does not make the effort or does not listen when we submit our translation to him” (p. 11).

1For a more comprehensive view of my position, see Spivak 2011.

Training and Supervising Analyst, Los Angeles Institute and Society for Psychoanalytic Studies.

Submitted for publication July 17, 2014.

1063
